title: Election Guide: What to know ahead of the 2026 Pennsylvania primary election
author: Jacob Godshall I The Daily Collegian
description:
On May 19, Pennsylvania’s primary elections will take place. This election gives voters a chance to decide which candidates will represent their political party in the general election in November.

May 4 is the deadline to register to vote in Pennsylvania primaries. May 12 is the last day to apply for an absentee or mail-in ballot. On May 19, polls will be open from 7 a.m. to 8 p.m.

Voting in the PA Primaries

Party Affiliation

Pennsylvania is one of eight states with a fully-closed primary election, meaning voters will only be able to vote in races within their own party.

Independent or unaffiliated voters will be unable to vote in either party’s primaries, but will still be able to answer ballot questions.

Polling Locations

Polling locations will be available throughout the state. Find your polling location here. For students living on campus, the polling location is in the HUB-Robeson Center.

What if I can’t vote in-person?

Any registered voter can apply for an absentee ballot if they are unable to make it to their polling location due to illness, disability, work or travel.

Alternatively, any registered voter may apply for a mail-in ballot. Mail-in ballots must be received by 8 p.m on May 19 and can be mailed back or dropped off at the polling location in person.

For more information on mail-in and absentee ballots, visit here.

Who can vote in the primaries?

Any Pennsylvania resident over the age of 18 who is registered to vote may participate in the 2026 primaries, with a few small exceptions.

Party Affiliations

The most restrictive exception, voters may only participate in their registered party’s primaries. Unaffiliated and third-party voters will still have the opportunity to vote on any ballot questions.

Age/Location Restrictions: To vote in any primary election, the voter must be 18 and resident of the state they plan to vote in (and the United States) for at least 30 days.

Felons: Most Pennsylvania voters with a felony will be able to vote in the primaries, but any who are currently in incarceration for a felony conviction will be unable to.

What do I need to bring?

If this is your first time voting at your polling location, you will need a valid ID from the list below.

This includes: driver’s licenses, U.S. passports, government issued IDs, student IDs, paychecks, bank statements, current utility bills, government issued checks or documents with full names and addresses, voter registration cards or firearm permits.

Who is up for election?

Governor

Current Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro is running unopposed in the Democratic primaries. He will be challenged by Republican Stacy Garrity, who is also running unopposed in the Republican primaries.

Shapiro has served as Pennsylvania’s governor since he was sworn in in 2023. He’s campaigning on public education investments, job creation and the representation of all Pennsylvanians, according to his campaign website.

Republican candidate Stacy Garrity, current state treasurer, has expressed support for natural gas drilling. Her campaign website lists her as a “strong ally” of President Donald Trump. She is campaigning on transparency, fraud elimination and cutting government waste.

15th congressional district

Both Democrat Ray Bilger and Republican Glenn Thompson are running unopposed in the primary race, and will face each other in November.

Bigler has expressed his personal connection to the area throughout his campaign, having grown up in Bellefonte. He is campaigning on affordability, increased health care and infrastructure. Additionally, he wants to bring “hope and opportunity” to the district, according to his campaign website.

Thompson has served as the representative from the 15th Congressional District since 2019. Before that, he was the representative from the 5th Congressional District from 2009 to 2018. Per his website, He is campaigning on supporting agriculture and farmers, rural development and increased infrastructure in rural areas.

82nd Legislative District

Both Paul Takac (D-Centre) and Republican Kirsten McTernan are running unopposed in the primary race, and will face each other in November.

Paul Takac has been the 82nd district’s representative since 2022. Takac is committed to clean and renewable energy in Pennsylvania, accessible health care and bringing resources to rural communities, according to his website.

After running for State College school board in 2025, McTernan will be running to represent the 82nd district in November. McTernan has not shared much publicly about her goals or priorities.

Member of Democratic State Committee

Candidates Laura Shadle, Joanne Totsi-Vasey and Jeremy S. Breon are running for re-election. Breon currently serves as the Prothonotary and Clerk of Courts, while Shadle and Totsi-Vasey are general members.

All three candidates will be elected to the committee, as three positions are available for this election.

Member of Republican State Committee

Candidates Rick Smead, Michelle Schellberg and Monica Oakes are running for re-election. Oakes currently serves as the secretary, Schellberg as the chair and Smead as a general member.

All three candidates listed on the ballot will be elected to the committee, as three positions are available for this election.

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Predicting 2026 Penn State defense’s post-spring practice depth chart
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State’s defense, just like its offense, has dealt with some injuries from the end of last season and into the spring practices that leaves the projected depth chart still unknown.

However, after 15 spring practices, here’s a prediction of the defensive depth chart heading into next season.

Defensive end

1. Yvan Kemajou, sophomore

2. Max Granville, redshirt sophomore

3. Alex McPherson, sophomore

4. Ike Ezeogu, redshirt senior

Penn State’s defensive end room still wasn’t at full strength at the end of spring practices, as Max Granville has been working back from multiple different injuries. However, the unit showed what the depth chart could look like.

Yvan Kemajou burned his redshirt last year as one of the key contributing freshmen on the team, proving himself as a reliable pass rusher. This year, he’ll enter as one of, if not the top pass rushers on the team, with the defensive end room holding a more youthful trend.

Granville is the other projected starter and very well could be the top pass rusher, but his production is strictly speculatory right now after he redshirted and spent all of last season recovering from a season-ending injury from last May.

Alex McPherson and Ike Ezeogu slot in as the two backups as two guys who’ve received more playing time in their previous schools than either Granville or Kemajou have so far at Penn State. Those two will provide more experience and allow for the pass rushers to rotate in with previous action at the college level.

Defensive tackle

1. Keanu Williams, redshirt senior

2. Armstrong Nnodim, redshirt sophomore

3. Siale Taupaki, redshirt senior +

4. Dallas Vakalahi, junior

Unlike the defensive end room, the defensive tackle group is flooded with older players. The two UCLA transfers, Siale Taupaki and Keanu Williams bring significant experience to the room, but they might not be the “starters.”

Williams brings the most production, starting in 11 of UCLA’s 12 games last year, totaling 39 tackles in 2025. Taupaki, on the other hand, didn’t start in as many games, but played in all 12, only notching two tackles for loss.

Armstrong Nnodim has been the riser in spring practice as a power rusher and disrupter of the group. His size matched with his ability to wreak havoc came alive at Blue-White Practice, making an impact in the 11-on-11 portion of the day. Following the practice, offensive lineman Cooper Cousins mentioned Nnodim as one of the players who talks the most trash on the line, proving his ferocious mentality.

Rounding out the group, Dallas Vakalahi is another defensive tackle who’s bigger, transferring from Utah where he played in nine games and recorded 11 tackles.

No matter who starts in every game, the four defensive tackles will all be contributors at a position that thrives more with added depth.

Linebacker

WILL:

1. Tony Rojas, redshirt junior

2. Cael Brezina, junior

MIKE:

1. Kooper Ebel, senior

2. Alex Tatsch, sophomore

SAM:

1. Caleb Bacon, redshirt senior

2. Rojas

The linebacker position is the group that was the most impacted by Iowa State transfers. However, Tony Rojas will still get the nod as the WILL linebacker when he returns from injury.

With Rojas missing spring practices, the three Iowa State linebackers — Cael Brezina, Caleb Bacon and Kooper Ebel — were all seen on the field together, with Brezina filling in Rojas’ starting spot.

Ebel will be the MIKE linebacker and will likely be the one to get the green dot, as he said after Blue-White Practice he’s been the one primarily wearing it. He’ll also potentially be the top linebacker on the team after tying for the lead in tackles at Iowa State last season.

Alex Tatsch is another player who’s dealt with injuries and will likely be out longer than the rest of the players who’re currently out. While he’s ahead of schedule, it’s expected for him to not be a full-go until much closer to Week 1, and slotting him as the next guy in line as the MIKE after a strong end to the 2025 season, fits him better than the other two linebacker positions.

Cornerback

Outside:

1. Audavion Collins, redshirt senior

2. Daryus Dixson, sophomore

3. Jahmir Joseph, redshirt freshman

Nickel:

1. Zion Tracy, senior

2. Omarion Davis, sophomore

The cornerback room is the one with the most familiarity from last season’s Penn State squad. Audavion Collins, Daryus Dixson and Jahmir Joseph all return as outside cornerbacks and will see a lot of playing time, with Collins and Dixson most likely getting the starting roles.

Collins emerged as one of the starters last year and proved he can hold up on the outside against Big Ten receivers. While Dixson may not be the top guy to start the year, he’ll still start every game and has the potential to be the go-to corner to put against opposing teams’ top targets.

Zion Tracy has received the most hype in the offseason, getting major praise from Campbell throughout spring practices. The nickel spot is his, but he’s someone who can do it all, potentially playing some outside or safety this season.

Joseph will play throughout the year as the next corner behind Collins and Dixson. While he didn’t receive as much playing time as Dixson did in their freshmen campaigns, his five games played were scattered throughout, as he maintained his redshirt before playing significantly more in the Pinstripe Bowl against Clemson.

Omarion Davis, despite being listed as a safety, could play in the nickel position when Tracy isn’t on the field. Davis totaled 13 tackles and a quarterback hurry in his freshman year at Boston College.

Safety

Strong safety:

1. Marcus Neal Jr., junior

2. Vaboue Toure, redshirt sophomore

Free safety:

1. Jeremiah Cooper, redshirt senior

2. Jamison Patton, senior

After the departure of Zakee Wheatley to the NFL draft and King Mack to NC State, the safety position will bring in mostly new faces.

Marcus Neal Jr. still holds the starting strong safety spot. Neal was tied for the lead in tackles with Ebel at Iowa State last season with 77, while also picking off two passes.

On the other side, Jeremiah Cooper will bring speed to the position, having played both cornerback and safety during his time with the Cyclones. In February, he confirmed he’ll be playing safety with the Nittany Lions, the position he had more success at with Iowa State.

Behind them, Vaboue Toure returns to Penn State for his redshirt sophomore season, having been behind multiple players who’re now in the NFL. Jamison Patton, while potentially not starting, will be a player who sees the field frequently in his final year.

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: From outfits to essentials, students share how they prepare for Movin’ On
author: Serina Chan I For the Collegian
description:
With the semester coming to a close, students are getting ready for Movin’ On, Penn State’s annual, student-run music festival set for Friday at the IM fields from 3:30 p.m. to 10:30 p.m.

Four national acts — Zara Larsson, the Dare, That Mexican OT and Slayyyter — and one local BATTLE winner will perform, meaning students get a range of music genres and styles to see at the event. While excitement is settling over campus, some students are also juggling the balance of academics right before finals week.

Ariana Canariato said she’s planning to study beforehand to ensure her academics come first, and her amusement comes second. In the past week leading up to the festival, she’s been making sure her work gets done.

“I think it’s definitely okay to take a break and go have fun, especially before finals week,” Ariana Canariato, a second-year studying veterinary and biomedical sciences, said.

Canariato also said she’s excited to dress up for the music festival, and that her planned outfit is inspired by headliner Zara Larsson. Larsson is in an era of bright colors and glittery costumes after her 2025 release, “Midnight Sun,” ignited a maximalist, sun-saturated aesthetic across social media — think Lisa Frank and Malibu Barbie.

“[I’m] maybe wearing some type of jean and making the outfit summery,” Canariato said. “I also have claw clips that fit the vibe of the concert.”

Emma Dorko also said the outfit she picked out is also inspired by Larsson. The overall concert atmosphere played a large role in her decision too.

“I think it’s going to be 50/50 in terms of whether people dress up or down,” Dorko, a fourth-year studying animal science, said. “I might wear jeans because it’s going to be colder. But I want to match myself to Zara Larsson’s concert.”

Issy Beck said she and her friends are definitely planning on dressing up in a style similar to that seen at Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival, which is as much about the fashion as it is the music.

“My friends and I would make the whole festival a huge thing,” Beck, a second-year studying biobehavioral health, said. “I would wear mini shorts, boots and a jersey that would be tucked in. I would also want to bring my digital camera.”

Olivia Yogus said she’s planned what emergency items to bring instead of her outfit ahead of time.

“I would bring a portable charger,” Yogus, a second-year studying biology, said. “I don’t know how long I’m going to be there for, so it would be good to have on me.”

Michael Noel, however, said he isn’t planning to bring anything besides his wallet and phone.

“Last year it rained,” Noel, a second-year studying mechanical engineering, said. “I don’t want to have a lot of stuff in my pockets that I could lose or fall out.”

Noel also said he’s dressing for comfort, not for style.

“It depends on the weather,” Noel said. “If it’s raining, I would wear a long sleeve shirt and jeans. I would dress the way I normally do.”

Samuel Endres said the mosh pit that usually opens up at the festival is influencing his outfits plans. He said the heat from all the bodies packed together can make wearing clothes typically meant for colder weather difficult.

“I’ll probably wear brown Carhartt pants, flip flops and a black t-shirt,” Endres, a second-year studying kinesiology, said. “If I get hot or if something gets on my shirt, it won’t be super noticeable if I dress simple.”

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Film review | Analyzing Penn State women’s basketball’s newest forwards Cristen Carter, Petra Bozan
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
As coach Tanisha Wright continues to build her squad at Penn State after starting with zero players due to the transfer portal and graduation, she added two valuable positions to end last week.

Wright added her first two forwards on her squad in Georgetown’s Cristen Carter and Nebraska’s Petra Božan on Friday and Saturday, completing a full lineup as Bozan became the fifth player on the team.

Both Carter and Bozan have several years of collegiate experience with the Cornhuskers, Hoyas and Bulldogs, creating a veteran presence amongst the Lady Lion forwards.

Cristen Carter

After starting 30 games between two seasons at Butler, 6-foot-4 Cristen Carter took her talents to Georgetown, averaging nearly just as many minutes in only five starts.

While Carter only averaged 4.6 points per game during her junior year, her scoring came with great playmaking.

Against one of the best teams in the nation in UConn, Carter cut to the basket wide open, receiving an assist to make the easy layup.

Georgetown transfer Cristen Carter evades the UConn defense, receiving an assist for a wide open layup under the basket pic.twitter.com/vtyCiMZDTI— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

Despite recording less than one assist per game in each collegiate season, Carter is formidable when passing the ball out to her teammates.

In a similar play, Carter found herself on the passing end against Maryland, dishing the ball over the defender to an open teammate for another score.

This time, Carter is the one passing the ball and dishes it to her open teammate in the paint against Maryland pic.twitter.com/HB86iFiTK0— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

During her time at Georgetown, Carter recorded just 12 blocks on the season. However, that number was nearly doubled in each of her seasons with Butler.

Against the Terrapins, the Indianapolis native jumped out of the lane to play on-ball defense. After the ball was passed to her matchup, she quickly rushed back between her and the basket with her arms up, smacking the ball out of the air.

Carter’s playmaking abilities extend to the defensive side, where she quickly moves back in position and denies the shot pic.twitter.com/YdxI59wnGI— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

Petra Božan

Fortunately for the Lady Lions, general manager and assistant coach Jason Crafton is the only returner who saw Božan’s career-high 17-point performance against Penn State last season.

The Croatia native has a slightly higher-scoring output than Carter, averaging 6.6 points per game. After coming off the bench in all 33 games her freshman year, Bozan saw a bigger role in Year 2, making 19 starts.

Against the blue and white, Bozan played a big role in the team’s only 100-point performance during the Big Ten schedule.

Bozan showcased her 6-foot-3 height right off the bat, jumping high to intercept a pass thrown at 6-foot-6 Gracie Merkle, which normally has no issues reaching her.

Nebraska transfer Petra Bozan earns a steal on the first play of the game against Penn State, intercepting a pass intended for 6-foot-6 Gracie Merkle pic.twitter.com/iMAH53PA3c— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

However, that wasn’t the only success she had against Merkle. After receiving the inbound under the basket, Bozan stepped back out of the paint and scored over Merkle, putting the first points on the board for either team.

Bozan also has offensive success against Merkle, scoring a step-back jumper over her tall frame pic.twitter.com/Vor7KcocPh— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

That physicality and finishing success were also on display later in the season as the Cornhuskers were battling Iowa on the road.

Separated by four points halfway through the final quarter, Bozan grabbed the rebound and converted on a second-chance opportunity, earning an opportunity at the free throw line as well.

Against Iowa, Bozan took full control of the play, securing the rebound, scoring the second-chance layup and earning the and-one opportunity pic.twitter.com/jOTlPp2oao— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball hosts No. 2 Nebraska for last regular season series
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is set to face its hardest opponent on the regular season schedule. No. 2 Nebraska will visit Happy Valley to close out the regular season with a Big Ten series.

The Nittany Lions have a current four-game winning streak, sweeping Illinois for their last road conference series, then defeating Saint Francis for their last nonconference opponent of the 2026 season.

This weekend’s meeting will be the 25th time the Cornhuskers and the blue and white will go head-to-head on the diamond in program history. Nebraska leads the overall series 16-8.

Nebraska is on a 15-game win streak, with a 40-6 overall record. The Cornhuskers sit at the first slot of the Big Ten conference, 20-1 in conference play with a 13-game win streak. The last loss Nebraska faced was in late March, losing to then No.7 UCLA in a final 6-5.

Rhonda Revelle is in her 34th season as the Cornhuskers head coach. With over 1,100 wins, Revelle has been the face of the program at her alma mater. Being a part of the inaugural year of the Women's College World Series in 1982, she was also named to the All-Big Eight Selection that same year.

Entering the 2026 season, Revelle owns the third-most wins of any active Division I coach.

Having a roster filled with young talent, Nebraska has three players over .400 batting average.

Right-handed senior pitcher Jordy Frahm is one of the elite in her graduating class. A Papillion, Nebraska, native is finishing her fifth season of eligibility. Winning the WCWS in her first two years at Oklahoma, Frahm decided to finish her college career in her home state.

She has more than thrived in the Cornhuskers uniform; the two-way player has a 1.30 ERA and .423 batting average in her final year. So far this season, she has been able to strike out 12 batters in a single game and has 17 home runs on the season. In 2025, Frahm was named NFCA DI Player of the Year and Big Ten Pitcher and Player of the Year.

The Nittany Lions won’t only see Frahm in the circle, but also left-hander Hannah Camenzind as well. The senior is a Valley, Nebraska, native who is also a dual player with a 1.40 ERA and a .408 batting average on the season.

Outfielder Alina Felix sits in first place in the Cornhuskers' batting average with a .455 in her senior season.

Frahm and Camenzind are both in the top ten of the Big Ten batters, but Frahm currently takes the first spot for pitchers in the conference.

The Nittany Lions roster has an aggressive approach in the batter’s box, with freshman infielder Allison Oneacre at the top of the offense with a .388 batting average and 17 homers in her inaugural season.

On the pitching side of the ball, righty McKenna Young leads the Nittany Lions with a 3.31 ERA, and freshman Mackenzie Duncan has the most strikeouts with 73 on the season.

As this will be the hardest opponent Penn State will face so far in their historic 2026 campaign, this upcoming series looks to be a challenging one for the blue and white before postseason play begins.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 15 Penn State men’s lacrosse tops No. 11 Maryland, advances to Big Ten championship
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 15 Penn State opened conference play against No. 11 Maryland on March 21, and started its postseason with a rematch against the Terrapins in the Big Ten semifinal.

The regular season meeting ended in favor of the Nittany Lions, as the blue and white secured a 10-6 victory in Happy Valley. Panzer Stadium was filled to the brim, with a venue-record 2,301 fans in attendance to watch Penn State’s first-ever home victory over Maryland.

On Thursday night, the familiar foes headed to a neutral site, Rutgers’ SHI Stadium, with a berth in the conference championship on the line. The blue and white grinded out an 8-6 win in a close, defensive battle.

Eric Spanos got the Terrapins on the board first, beating his man off the dodge and cutting down the alley before whipping the ball just over Preston Hawkins’ head and into the Nittany Lion nylon.

Most of the opening quarter consisted of the teams’ traded defensive plays, until the blue and white finally got on the board when Michael Faraone dunked one home with 4:54 left in the frame. The scoreless run marked the longest game-opening drought all season for Penn State.

It wouldn’t take long for it to get another, however, as attackman Hunter Aquino faked his defender out and rolled around the crease to slam a close shot home, putting Penn State up 2-1 late in the frame. He’d get his second towards the midpoint of the second frame, giving Penn State a two-goal advantage.

After scoring the first goal of the game less than three minutes into the opening quarter, Maryland wouldn’t find the back of the net again in the first half. Strong defense on both sides brought the game into the break with the blue and white ahead 3-1.

Penn State’s defensive stubbornness held coach John Tillman’s offense to an uncharacteristically low output, as the lone goal marked Maryland’s lowest first-half total all season.

Early fouls on Jack Iannantuono and Jon King gave the Terrapins a two-man advantage, and attackman Leo Johnson capitalized quickly with a close, far-side shot that beat Hawkins to end the nearly 30 minute scoring drought.

Just after the Maryland score, the Nittany Lions’ defense came up with a stop, and defenseman Will Costin drove upfield with the ball in his stick. A late slide allowed the 6-foot-1 defender to get his hands free, wind up and power his second goal of the season into the net.

On the next Penn State possession, quick passing worked Aquino free, and he fired a lefty shot into the top-right corner of the net to seal his hat trick.

Maryland got one back not long after, when Johnson fired his second goal of the night to bring the Nittany Lion advantage to two goals at the third quarter’s media timeout. The lead would hold until the end of the quarter, with the blue and white up 5-3 as the buzzer sounded.

A close call on the goal line warranted a challenge from Tillman, but the initial call of no goal stood, and the Terrapins lost a timeout. On the ensuing possession, Mac Hobbs drove towards the crease and was pushed into it, putting Penn State man-up in a crucial situation.

As Luke Walstrum worked from X, Liam Matthews settled on the crease. Walstrum caught Maryland ball-watching and fed the Canadian, who flicked it past Terrapin netminder Brian Ruppel.

Despite being down late, Maryland kept at it. Spanos drew a slide and found Jack Schultz, who was wide open for an easy score on a delayed penalty.

The Terrapins got another quick, slick goal two minutes later when Chris Lyons hitched past a Penn State defender and fed Braden Erksa to narrow the lead to just one.

On the consequent faceoff, Ryan O’Connor scooped the ball off the turf and flicked an underhand pass to Jon King, who ran down the throat of the Maryland defense and fired a low shot past Ruppel.

Spanos found the net again when he powered past his defender and whipped a sidearm shot past Hawkins’right leg. The goal brought the score to a 7-6 Nittany Lions lead with 2:40 to go in the game.

With seconds draining off the clock, reigning Big Ten Freshman of the Week Andrew Beard beat his defender and finished on goal to deliver the game-sealing goal. Maryland had a final offensive chance, but a save from Hawkins allowed the blue and white to hang on to their lead until the end.

Up next

With their victory on Thursday, the Nittany Lions advance to Saturday’s Big Ten championship, where they’ll face No. 7 Johns Hopkins. Faceoff is set for noon at SHI Stadium.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: McKenna added to Team Canada roster ahead of World Hockey Championship
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
Gavin McKenna is headed to the international stage, earning a spot on Team Canada’s roster for the 2026 World Hockey Championship.

Gavin McKenna added to Canada’s roster ahead of the 2026 World Hockey Championship in Switzerland on May 15-31!! 🏒🇨🇦Read ➡️ https://t.co/HWQHuiGFyb#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/WW0HcFxw2l— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) April 30, 2026

McKenna gets the call after a strong first season with the Nittany Lions.The White Horse, Yukon, native earned the nod after a record-setting freshman season where he totaled 51 points.

The forward's point total set a Penn State freshman record, and his 36 assists set a single-season program record.

The tournament is set for May 15–31, where 16 teams will compete for the title in Zurich and Fribourg, Switzerland.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball returns home for conference series against Minnesota
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions return to Happy Valley after an unsuccessful trip out west. Penn State was unable to earn a victory in the three-game series against then-No. 19 Oregon, ultimately suffering a sweep as the season nears its end. After its game against #18 West Virginia was canceled due to rain, attention now turns to another Big Ten opponent in Minnesota for the blue and white.

With only two home series remaining, upcoming games pose as must-wins for Penn State if it wants to hold a Big Ten tournament spot.

Recent games have favored the Nittany Lions, who are on a three-game win streak against the Gophers, with their last game ending in a high-scoring 15-10 victory. However, both teams have had similar seasons as they currently hold losing conference records.

Pitching troubles seemed to flip for Penn State coming off the series loss to the Ducks. Two out of three starters went six innings and combined for 17 strikeouts. Colin Fitzgerald had a game to remember after recording his career-high 12 punchouts in a game and only allowing three earned runs.

The seemingly rejuvenated pitching staff goes against a Minnesota offense that ranks 10th in the Big Ten. The Golden Gophers’ lineup has been doing very well at the plate as of late, earning over 10 runs in two of their last four games.

Weber Neels leads his team with a .359 batting average and 12 home runs. The Cottage Grove, Minnesota, native has five homers in his last seven games and has been a key contributor in the Gophers five-game win streak.

A hot offense has been backed up by a pitching staff that hasn’t allowed over three runs in its last five games. Minnesota’s pitching currently ranks top-five in the Big Ten with a 4.17 team ERA. Starting pitcher Marcus Kruzan leads the Minnesota starters with a 3.00 ERA. The junior has allowed six earned runs in his last six games while striking out 20 total batters.

Additionally, the Golden Gophers' bullpen has been able to keep wins intact in recent games. Five relievers currently have below a 3.00 ERA heading into the weekend.

However, the pitching staff will face an improving Penn State lineup. Bryce Molinaro used the weekend to continue staying on track in the season. The Drums, Pennsylvania, native hit a homer in his recent series, earning a total of 3 RBIs.

Michael Anderson continues to be a problem for pitchers. Although not recording an RBI over the weekend, the senior still sits tied at first in home runs in the Big Ten, continuing his dominant first season in the blue and white.

Penn State will need its bats to stay hot against a Minnesota team that has been lighting up the scoreboard and can keep an opposing lineup quiet. A series win could boost confidence for the Nittany Lions entering their last seven games of the season.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State picks up commitment from 4-star Stanley Montgomery
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Ikaika Malloe stayed in Pennsylvania for his latest addition to the defensive line in the Class of 2027, picking up a 4-star recruit from Philadelphia.

Stanley Montgomery committed to Penn State on Thursday, he announced via X.

Montgomery is the No. 7 player out of Pennsylvania and No. 24 defensive lineman in his class, per 247Sports composite.

He was offered to be a Nittany Lion on Jan. 15 while already committed to Syracuse, the first school to make him an official offer. However, Montgomery decommitted from the Orange on Feb. 3. He later chose Penn State over Kentucky, Rutgers and Virginia with an official visit to Happy Valley set for June 5.

In his junior season with Archbishop Ryan High School, Montgomery tallied 45 tackles including eight sacks and 19 tackles for loss, totaling 84 yards.

The 6-foot-3, 300-pounder was named First Team All-Catholic for both offensive and defensive line duties, aiding the Raiders in capturing the Philadelphia Catholic League Blue Division football championship for the second year in a row.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball junior Brooke Klosowicz tabbed as top utility player
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's softball Brooke Klosowicz named a top-five utility player by Softball America

one of the nation’s best🎱#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/wCXZgwZijh— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) April 30, 2026

The Nittany Lion 2026 squad has done it again, getting their third honor just this week. This time it was veteran junior Brooke Klosowicz.

Softball America announced Thursday afternoon D1 softball's top utility players of this year, with the Johnsburg, Illinois, native getting the fourth slot in the rankings.

So far, Klosowicz has a .357 batting average and 0.881 fielding percentage in her junior season.
