title: Penn State football draft profile | OL Vega Ioane’s high character, power key to success at NFL
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
After Penn State’s fifth straight loss, this one coming at the hands of Ohio State, Vega Ioane didn’t have his head down or sulk, instead, he was seen pushing an equipment cart up a ramp, helping a staff member.

That type of character and his on-field play is why Ioane will likely hear his name at some point on Thursday of the NFL draft.

After starting in five games his redshirt freshman season, Ioane earned the starting left guard role as a redshirt sophomore. His blocking was a reason for the Nittany Lions’ running success en route to a College Football Playoff semifinal appearance.

Ioane started every game his last two seasons, and was often used as a gadget player in the versatile playbook of former offensive coordinator Andy Kotelnicki, sometimes being used as a blocker in motion to lay the boom on an edge rusher.

“My four years here, that biggest adversity I've ever faced, you know, I was 2000 miles away from home, took a chance on being here growing up as a man,” Ioane said at pro day. “It's exactly what Penn State did for me. Came to campus as a kid, left as a man.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-4

Weight: 326 pounds

Vertical jump: 31.5 inches

Broad jump: 8-foot-8

Projections

Projected round drafted: Round 1

Player comparison: Tyler Booker

Scouting report

Ioane stands out on the offensive line. In a unit that was pretty strong the past two years, he was the anchor, the mauler and the protector, no matter what the play call was.

His power has allowed him to fend off some of the top linemen in the Big Ten, protecting Drew Allar from interior threats. However, given his size, his speed isn’t necessarily there despite his usage as the gadget lineman.

What he lacks in speed he makes up in footwork, though. Ioane’s flexibility is also top-tier, and he was never reliant on Kotelnicki’s scheme, which makes him an even better prospect, as any team could feel comfortable taking Ioane.

Ioane’s rise into the first round came after dominant 2024-25 seasons as a pass blocker, not allowing a single sack despite over 800 pass-blocking snaps. He also didn’t pick up a single holding flag.

Part of the reason his pass-blocking is so effective is his sturdy feet. Defenders can’t get past him because he doesn’t waver and anchors himself into the grass, using his strength to keep pass rushers at bay.

Although Ioane only started games at left guard, he’s taken snaps at every position on the offensive line and has been training this draft process so he’s prepared to play center if necessary. Ioane also had a stint at right guard last spring before JB Nelson transferred.

Best team fit: Pittsburgh Steelers

The Pittsburgh Steelers have a glaring hole at left guard. After losing Isaac Seumalo in free agency to the Arizona Cardinals, Spencer Anderson is the presumed starter.

Anderson played a good role as an extra offensive lineman, and has held his own when needing to fill in, but question marks arise if he’d be able to last and hold it down as a consistent lineman on the field.

Ioane would immediately slot in as the starting left guard and likely thrive next to center Zach Frazier, adding to the young but strong offensive line that the Steelers have built through the draft.

The big question is if Ioane will be available at pick No. 21. Recent mock drafts have him going as high as No. 14 to the Baltimore Ravens, so if the Steelers want the top interior offensive lineman in the NFL draft, they’ll likely have to move up to take him, or hope he somehow falls into their lap.

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: Senior Column | Ugly roads lead to beautiful destinations
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Slightly over a month into my freshman year, I got sick. It felt like a cold, so I treated it as such. But when Central Michigan came to town on Sept. 24, 2022, I had someone tell me I looked green in Beaver Stadium.

The next day, I was diagnosed with pneumonia at University Health Services.

As all good moms do, mine immediately drove to State College and got an Airbnb to try to nurse me back to health. I applaud your efforts, Mom, but I was a wreck, violently coughing, had a spiking fever and consistently went from having chills to freakishly sweating.

That Friday, we moved to a different Airbnb, one that had been purchased months prior as my younger brother, Will, and my dad visited for the Northwestern football game. They went to it because I convinced them I was fine, but in reality, I was hacking up a lung and felt like death.

Not sure why I told them to go, or why they listened to me, but we can move on. Either way, it’s one hell of a way for the family to visit you at college for the first time.

On Sunday, I moved into Mount Nittany Medical Center, where I said goodbye to my mom and Will while having several needles injecting me with IVs and fluids. My oxygen was low, and my white blood cells were crazily spiking.

After six days of no sleep, taking insane amounts of medications and my dad running around State College getting me food so I didn’t have to eat the hospital stuff, I made it out. After one more sleep in a different Airbnb, I returned home to Briarcliff Manor, New York, to recover for a few days.

Then I returned to school, my lungs permanently damaged and still coughing a ton. From November to winter break (with a case of the flu the week before Thanksgiving, where I went home again), my days were spent going to class and coming back to Geary Hall, sleeping and slamming antibiotics.

By April, I was feeling somewhat like myself. I made some friends and covered a few baseball games for The Daily Collegian after not doing anything in the fall on the women’s soccer beat. I returned home for the summer and somehow passed all my classes.

I had a great summer, spending time with family and friends from home. I was still operating on tons of medication and had countless doctor appointments, but I felt normalish.

One afternoon, I was going on a walk around my neighborhood with my Mom, reflecting on my freshman year. I admitted that it sucked, mainly pointing at my health as the reason. She asked if I had thought about transferring. I immediately said no.

Truth be told, I had thought about it a lot my freshman year. I thought about it when I returned that fall, in the spring and that summer.

The reason was I didn’t know what my social life would look like. I had some friends, but despite hanging out with them, part of me felt like I was the kid clinging on. When I was sick, people were finding roommates for the following year, so I was forced to go with a random on-campus roommate. Everyone I hung out with would live together off-campus, and that worried me.

I never said any of this until a few days before I left for sophomore year, where instead of talking it out with my family, I broke down crying at the dinner table, saying I didn’t want to leave my friends at home.

Eventually, I made my way to school, scared out of my mind. My random roommate, Colin Baverso, turned out great, and while we didn’t hang out after that year, it’s always nice running into him. My friends that I was scared weren’t actually my friends are some of the closest friends I have to this day. I also made new friends.

My sophomore year rocked. My grades went up, my social life took a step forward and my health was turning a corner.

That joy was largely due to the Collegian. I didn’t really do anything my freshman year, so I sometimes wonder how I made staff again, but I’m glad I did. I decided to become more invested in the paper, returning to the sports staff covering women’s soccer.

I didn’t have a passion for sports journalism before that fall, but I most certainly found it. I loved that season covering women’s soccer, writing everything I could, earning reporter of the semester for sports staff and switching my major from economics to digital and print journalism.

But I wanted more. Covering women’s soccer and becoming an update merchant for other beats wasn’t enough. I joined women’s basketball and men’s lacrosse, doing anything I could to write and contribute to the Collegian. The office became a home. The people became a family.

By the end of the year, I earned reporter of the semester again, and was honored to be selected for the illustrious football staff, ready to grow on the beat that so many talented reporters come through. After two practices and the Blue-White Game, I was hooked as I headed home for the summer.

Another walk with my mom that summer brought up another question, where she asked me if I wanted to do football since my social life took off, with a big part of that being tailgating in the fall. I deflected, but like the last walk, it was something I thought about a lot.

In reality, I’d been doing a lot of thinking, but not just about that.

While my life had taken off on paper, that entire sophomore year, I didn’t bring up my life-altering freshman year. Why would I? It sucked. Nobody at school was there for it. Nobody saw and understood what I went through. So, I keep it to myself.

I never really talked about it with my friends from school or home. I never really talked about it with my family either.

That summer, my parents could tell something was wrong with me, though I didn’t know what was wrong with me. One afternoon, and I couldn’t tell you how it happened, I let it all out.

I cried again, but I also unleashed every single bottled-up emotion and thing that needed to be said over the last two years. I couldn’t tell you the specifics of what was said, but what I learned was that I had mental health issues going on.

I hate saying “mental health issues” which ended up being part of the issue. I never really wanted to talk about my past, because to me, it seemed like the soft thing to do. Stupid as hell, I know.

Before I left for junior year, I talked with my family a lot and had some professional conversations. It took a bit, with a handful of emotional moments in between, but I entered junior year in a good headspace, having cleared a lot out mentally.

Once I settled into University Gateway Apt. 406, my home for the next two years alongside AJ Piazza and Luke Sheluck, I talked about what went down that summer. They listened and were understanding. Shocker. That was all I needed, to be honest.

A week later, I found myself in West Virginia to kick off covering football, leaving at 5 a.m. and not getting back to school till midnight due to a three-hour delay of thunder and lightning.

Football was everything I wanted and then some. I went to West Virginia, Wisconsin, Purdue, Minnesota and Indianapolis for the Big Ten Championship, Arizona for the Fiesta Bowl and Florida for the Orange Bowl in a season that Penn State was a drive away from playing for the national championship. Those were the best months of my life alongside the best psufootblog in Collegian history with Joel Haas, Lexie Linderman and Avery Hill.

And another shocker — during and after the season, I hung out with my non-Collegian friends despite not tailgating.

Junior year was better than sophomore year. I briefly had a run on the photo staff for some reason. I turned 21, where I was introduced to Doggies, the greatest place on earth.

Senior year came around in the blink of an eye, where Will Horstman joined the football crew with Joel exhausting eligibility. Joel got employed and still covers Penn State, so he’s just Unc now and essentially never left.

The season was different, as a national championship caliber team turned into James Franklin being fired, a 7-6 record and the hiring of Matt Campbell. I went to UCLA, Iowa, Ohio State, Michigan State and Rutgers. The season capped off with the Pinstripe Bowl at Yankee Stadium, where I’ve been going to Yankees’ games since I was a little kid as a fan, so a pretty cool way to cover my last football game.

I churned out articles, picked up a wrestling editor position somewhere in between, working with a bunch of hooligans in AJ, Andrew Deal and Evan Smith. A lethal group to say the least, but an elite one at that, and an even more elite group chat.

I continued to write, edit, podcast, occasionally do a newsletter and successfully avoid making pages, but my time is up as my responsibilities are not covering Penn State sports, but filling out job applications on TeamWork Online.

I want to thank sports journalism. It gave me a passion for something I didn’t know I had. I’ve always loved sports, but I didn’t know I loved writing this much. My English grades have always been good, but I didn’t know this was where writing could take me.

I love storytelling. I love talking to athletes and coaches. I love going on camera and talking about Penn State football. I love working so hard on a story that, in the end, I’m proud of it.

I got a byline writing for the Associated Press covering the Super Bowl this year. I got to travel all over the country to see college campuses, cover a College Football Playoff run and be in four NFL stadiums. I got to cover a coach being fired and a coach being hired. I wrote for the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette. I wrote for the Cape Cod Baseball League for the championship-winning Bourne Braves.

I gained over 3,700 followers on X, and had someone come up to me at Champs while I was double-fisting dirty sprites to tell me how much he loved reading my work and following me.

I asked questions to Franklin, Campbell, Drew Allar, Abdul Carter, Nick Singleton, Tyler Warren, Terry Smith, Sam Darnold, Steffon Diggs, Cooper Kupp, Jaxon Smith-Njigba, Rece Davis, Desmond Howard, Jarrod Saltalamachia, Gavin McKenna, Cael Sanderson and more.

I watched Jaxson Dart and Cam Skattebo eat wings in front of me. I watched Justin Jefferson and Michael Vick play Madden together. Drake Maye, Christian McCaffrey, Ashton Jeanty, Fernando Mendoza, Pat McAfee, Nick Saban, Stephen A. Smith, Deion Sanders, George Kittle, Curt Cignetti, George Kittle, Rick Pitino and Tom Izzo have been right in front of me.

Journalism is a horrendous industry. Sports journalism is a horrendous industry. I’m aware of that, hence why I’m keeping my professional options open. I think my resume, paired with close to 1,200 bylines with the Collegian, on top of being pretty good at what I do, makes me qualified for whatever professional job, but that’s not how the world works.

If this is my last byline, I wouldn’t want it to be for any other outlet. The Collegian has poured so much into me with all these opportunities and awesome people I’ve met. Back when I was unsure of how my sophomore year would look, the Collegian was there for me when nothing else was.

I hope I left it in a better place. Nothing brings me more joy than helping out others on the sports staff, hearing how excited people are about their stories and coverage and hanging out at the office where I’ve spent more time than my own apartment. People truly don’t realize what a special place it is. For anyone who has ever talked shit about the Collegian, respectfully, fuck you. You don’t know what you have.

Thank you to those that the Collegian has brought me — Evan, Will, Myles Hannak, Chase Mitchell, Zach Karpf, Kaitlyn Murphy, Kate Hildebrand, Adam Waxman, Sam Woloson, Joe Tuman, Olivia Estright, Seth Engle, Liam Wichser, Daniel Mader, Tyler Millen and more.

Thank you to the various versions of the “Where is Lyle” groupchat. Thank you for being my friends after I stupidly thought at one point that none of you really were. I no longer look worried.

Thank you to my hometown friends. Though the group chat isn’t active unless we’re all home, I love you all to death.

Andrew, you’re the man. It’s been awesome getting to know you and watching you grow the past two years. I’m fired up for you to be on football and know you’ll thrive. There aren’t a lot of people who truly pour so much into the Collegian, but you are most certainly one of them, and I am forever grateful for that.

Greg, it’s been a pleasure becoming your friend despite our first conversations over text. I thought you were a grade below me. You’re one of the nicest people I’ve ever met and you never have a bad thing to say about anybody. Though I’ll never forget the time a certain reporter fooled you into saying they were working on a feature the whole semester, just for them to then quit. I enjoyed your reaction to that. You’re one of a few people who I can truly talk sports journalism with, but also have even deeper conversations about Panda Express.

Joel, last year you said you didn’t write enough about me, so I’ll take this opportunity to do so about you. Thank you for being my first sports and football editor. I look up to you a lot in this sports journalism thing, and I appreciate all the guidance. If I’m not on the beat next year, you will now become my primary source of Penn State football content, and know you’ll continue to kill it in covering Penn State, moving heavy weights and Clash Royale. Thanks for letting me sit in your seat this year, but also for being a role model.

Lexie, despite you initially hating me without ever saying a word to me, and becoming a bit to your friends, you turned out alright. I hope I did too. I couldn’t ask for a better person to cover games with the last two years and make fun of for quite literally anything, but I’ll pick on you for not liking food and being gullible for the sake of time. You’re an incredible person and can’t wait for you to shine as editor, and for everyone to see what I know you’re capable of.

Avery, it’s been one hell of a ride. From you editing my horrendous baseball stories in the summer, to the Bryce Jordan Center, to Beaver Stadium, to covering the Super Bowl, it’s been a journey. You’re as talented as they come, but also an awesome person who knows as much ball as me. Being on the same beat the last three years has been a blessing, and hopefully one day we will work together as professionals in an area that has no string pins in its bowling alleys.

To the Other Team, you know who you are. You know what they say. Why not us? It was always. I’ll add you both are some of my best friends to add a wholesome component here.

AJ and Luke, thank you for being goated roommates for the last two years. The Kegway will never be the same once we’re done with it. Thank you for putting up with my horrendous coughing when it strikes. You both are incredible people and some of my best friends. Thank you for appreciating my love for halal cart — the only acceptable halal cart in State College.

Nana, grandma and grandpa Bill, thank you for always being in my corner. It means the world to have you all as a support system, and I wouldn’t have it any other way. Please continue to root for Penn State over Illinois for the foreseeable future, despite me graduating and Will still being in school.

Mom and dad, thank you for everything. You guys have been my rocks for as long as I’ve been alive and I’m forever grateful. The amount you have both done for me, and put up with has been a lot, especially over the last couple of years, but I’m incredibly appreciative. I can’t really put into words the impact you’ve both had on my life, but hopefully this will suffice.

Will, I still can’t believe you picked Illinois over Penn State, which means I don’t have a place to stay when I visit State College for the next couple of years. That being said, I’m looking forward to finally getting to campus after I get this photo tour done. Thank you for being my best friend and being my favorite person since you were born. Fired up to see what you accomplish and what’s down the road for us.

It’s time to wrap this up. The name of this column is “Ugly roads lead to beautiful destinations.” I felt like it was pretty fitting.

Making it to this enjoyable part in my life wasn’t pretty. Not the sickness. Not the doubt. Not the sleepless nights where I questioned everything. Most of the road to get to where I am was ugly.

But those moments led me here. To the people and experiences I wouldn’t trade for anything.

So if this is the end of my time at Penn State and the Collegian, I’ll leave it with this:

Ugly roads lead to beautiful destinations. This one was worth every step.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Film review | Scouting new Penn State women’s basketball commits Ava Black, Kamrah Banks
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After Tanisha Wright was hired as the next coach at Penn State on March 19, all 10 of the Lady Lions' returners entered the transfer portal.

Starting from scratch, Wright earned her first two commitments this week in 4-star incoming freshman Kamrah Banks and former Vanderbilt freshman Ava Black.

While both are younger pieces to Penn State’s squad, they offer a solid starting point in a rebuilding team with big moments early in their careers. Here’s a look at what the two new guards have to offer in Happy Valley.

Kamrah Banks

After committing to UCF due to a strong bond with the coaching staff, Banks reopened her recruitment after head coach Sytia Messer was fired in March, and ultimately landed as the first player at Penn State.

Throughout her time at Crispus Attucks High School, Banks showcased her abilities as a high-volume scorer. The 5-foot-9 guard left Indianapolis as the school’s all-time leading scorer, also holding a record for the most points in a game, breaking NBA Hall of Famer Oscar Robertson’s previous record.

Against North Central High School in December, Banks put on a dominant 30-point performance with four rebounds, three assists and two steals.

Despite being a guard, Banks showed no hesitation driving to the basket, scoring against pressure from the opposing center.

Against North Central High School, Banks takes the ball from the top of the key to the basket, bouncing off a defender for the score pic.twitter.com/In5ZjiWoJW— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

While she only hit two 3-pointers against the Panthers, she tallied 43 made triples her senior year, with an even-better 48% mark from deep in her junior year.

Banks shows no hesitation pulling up from deep, sinking one of her two 3-pointers against North Central pic.twitter.com/VGydfDQA6q— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

While she was known throughout her high school career as a high scorer, Banks was also efficient, dishing the ball, averaging 2.7 assists per game during the 2025-26 season.

Banks’ physicality likely would’ve aided her in scoring many of these opportunities. However, her IQ and vision led to an easier score with an assist to her teammate.

Banks shows strong court vision against the Panthers, finding an open teammate for an easy score pic.twitter.com/kvSGY3bWyi— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

Ava Black

Despite playing just 10.2 minutes per game, Black had several big games throughout her first year.

Her biggest game came at the biggest moment, when No. 2-seed Vanderbilt hosted No. 15-seed High Point in the first round of the NCAA Tournament. Black had a career day, scoring 12 points with three rebounds, an assist and a steal.

While Black plays as a bigger guard, standing at 5-foot-11, she still displayed the skillset of any guard, including being a 3-point threat.

Vanderbilt transfer Ava Black found herself open on the 3-point line for her second straight basket, sinking the triple to extend Vanderbilt’s dominant lead pic.twitter.com/OHUefex4jQ— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

However, one of the Auburn, New Hampshire, native’s best qualities comes in her playmaking, as she helps kickstart the Commodore offense.

After being fed the ball at the top of the key, Black dished it back to her teammate and found the open crease to the basket, receiving the ball and scoring on the way.

Black’s playmaking was on display with a give-and-go opportunity, finding herself running toward the basket for the wide-open score pic.twitter.com/bvifd82xMd— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

Black’s skill sets extend beyond her offensive scoring, as she also displayed success with on-ball defense.

The newest Lady Lion played defense at the top of the key, stealing the ball and making her way to the other end of the court for a fast-break layup.

Black’s success showed up on defense too, with fast-break steal and score to extend her career day in the NCAA Tournament pic.twitter.com/alwMeMT9oB— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

MORE BASKETBALL COVERGAE

title: ‘I’ve never seen anyone like her’ | Freshman phenom Rocquette Allen wows with Penn State women's lacrosse
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After committing to Syracuse in 2024, Rocquette Allen flipped to Penn State, following coach Kayla Treanor to Happy Valley.

With the Nittany Lions, Allen put on one of the most impressive freshman campaigns in the nation, earning numerous awards during her standout season.

“She’s just a phenomenal kid,” Treanor told The Daily Collegian. “She’s just so incredibly hardworking and super competitive. She’s just a really special kid.”

While she’s currently one of the best freshman players in the country, Allen didn’t grow up playing lacrosse. Instead, she began as a swimmer and finally picked up a lacrosse stick in middle school, traveling the country as she progressed in her early career.

“Honestly, in my opinion, swimming isn’t as fun anymore, just swimming back and forth,” Allen told the Collegian. “There’s no creativity compared to lacrosse. So my Dad bought me a real stick and I absolutely fell in love with it.”

After playing for her middle school team, Allen joined coach Aliyah Weant at Mountain Vista High School and traveled across the country during the offseason to practice and play with SkyWalkers lacrosse in Maryland.

Since Allen lived in Colorado, lacrosse was a smaller sport and didn’t have a lot of competition. With the SkyWalkers, she got to play in the capital of lacrosse.

“The opportunity for her to go play out on the East Coast was huge because the competition she saw there was a shocker,” Weant told the Collegian. “Her progression over the summer was significant.”

With the SkyWalkers, Weant noticed the effect of playing alongside other high-level athletes had on Allen, as the blooming prospect returned for each school season with a greater skillset.

At the same time, Allen began to be recruited by several schools, including Syracuse, by Treanor.

“I remember the first time watching her play and just thinking, ‘Oh my God, I love this kid,’” Treanor said. “She was electric to watch. She was a true crease attacker.”

As Allen continued to play with both her high school team and with the SkyWalkers, she showcased her work ethic and continued to set herself apart from her teammates.

“Roc is consistent,” Weant said. “Every time she shows up to a game, you know you’re going to get the best out of her. She’s consistent and her stick skills are unmatched. I’ve never seen anyone like her, ever in my career.”

During her junior year, she committed to Syracuse, a perennial national championship contender for women’s lacrosse. When Treanor made the move to Happy Valley, so did Allen.

“I’m just a huge fan of hers,” Treanor said. “I loved her in the recruiting process, so I was just thrilled that she came with me to Penn State.”

Arriving to Happy Valley in the fall, Allen quickly returned to the hard work and hustle she had put on display throughout her high school days.

“I’m not thinking about the future, it’s just how I can help my team out today,” Allen said. “I can always do better, I can always work harder, be better at serving everyone.”

As the season started, Allen was one of three Nittany Lions named as Big Ten Players to Watch and eventually started in all 17 games for the blue and white.

After scoring two of Penn State’s 10 goals in the season opener, the Lonetree, Colorado, native recorded her first of many five-goal games against UMass four days later.

“(Her performance) wasn’t surprising to me at all,” Weant said. “She’s just going to be back at the practice field working for the next one.”

Allen experienced her second five-goal game against Bucknell, followed by a lower-scoring game where she only scored two goals against Pitt. However, her second goal came at the most crucial time, defeating the Panthers in overtime.

“The thing that stood out to me most was her finishing ability behind the net,” Treanor said. “No angle is still a great angle for Rocquette Allen, and she’s one of the first players that I’ve seen play that I’m like, 'Ooh, that was similar to how I played.’”

The freshman star continued to battle through the season, earning her first Big Ten Freshman of the Week honor after scoring a goal with three assists against Drexel.

That was her first of many awards throughout the season as she continued to stuff the stat sheet. Midway through the season, Allen led the Nittany Lions in goals scored, making SportsCenter-caliber highlight plays in the process.

“I put the work in outside of practice,” Allen said. “Knowing that I’m working outside of practice and practicing those things, it’s all going to translate. Perfect practice makes perfect.”

Allen finished her freshman regular season with her sixth hat trick of the year, tallying 41 goals with 22 assists in Year 1. The young star also recorded her sixth Big Ten Freshman of the Week honors, capping off her season with a unanimous All-Big Ten Freshman Team selection.

“This year alone she’s had a lot of success,” Treanor said. “Knowing Roc, it’s only going to continue to climb. Just when you think that’s it for her, there’s always going to be something.”

While Allen wants to succeed and win a national championship during her four years at Penn State, her focus is on her day-to-day actions and being the best she can be.

“All I care about is God, winning and just being a better person,” Allen said. “I am very grateful for (awards) and everything I did to get those, but at the end of the day, there’s more to give and more to prove.”

title: Report: Penn State men's basketball guard Dominick Stewart transfers to Richmond
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Nittany Lion guard has reportedly found his next team.

Richmond has landed Penn State transfer Dominick Stewart, according to Jeff Goodman of The Field of 68. He has two seasons of eligibility remaining.

Richmond has landed Penn State transfer Dominick Stewart, his agent Ryan Murphy told @TheFieldOf68. The 6-5 sophomore averaged 6.6 points per game last season.— Jeff Goodman (@GoodmanHoops) April 23, 2026

Stewart took on a larger role for the Nittany Lions this past season, playing 22.5 minutes per game and starting in 24-of-32 appearances. He averaged 6.6 points, 2.2 rebounds and 1.6 assists per game with 40.1% shooting from the field.

Despite being a shooter on offense, Stewart only knocked down 34.8% of his shots from 3-point range in 2025-26.

However, he was praised for his perimeter defense by Mike Rhoades this past season, particularly after Penn State's win over Iowa for his efforts against guard Bennett Stirtz.

“He's our most physical perimeter defender, and with his length and how he could cover ground,” Rhoades said on March 2. “With the experience and getting all the reps guarding good players, he's getting better and better guarding and defending and understanding how to guard different players, honoring the scouting report."

Stewart was a 247Sports composite 3-star recruit in the 2024 class, averaging 2.8 points and 1.1 rebounds per game as a freshman in a limited role. Now, Stewart will compete in the A-10 as his collegiate career continues.

title: 'Healing happens together' | Honoring Survival event supports survivors, raises awareness
author: Emma Cherubini I The Daily Collegian
description:
The Relationship Violence Outreach, Intervention and Community Education Center’s (R-VOICE) hosted its annual Honoring Survival event at the Pasquerilla Spiritual Center Memorial Lounge on Wednesday to offer campus community and support for sexual assault survivors.

The event was from 3 to 5 p.m. and featured music, food and a drawing activity for participants. R-VOICE and Centre Safe co-hosted this year's event, both having tables where stickers, fidget toys, bracelets and sign ups for volunteering and a weekly newsletter were displayed.

Erin Carter has a background in art therapy counseling and said since being with the Penn State community for years, she sees how art is healing.

“I want to be able to allow others to use art in a healing way and community events like this are a great way to do that,” Carter, an assistant teaching professor of creative arts therapies, said.

The main community activity at this year's event was the chance for survivors to release themselves through art. Posters, papers, markers, colored pencils and magazine cutouts were all available to anyone in attendance to work with others and create art from words.

Megan Hutchens is a local therapist in Centre County and explained how, with her experience in working with clients who have experienced sexual assault in any capacity, finding that support in your community is important.

“Outreach is always important, getting the word out that there are people available to support,” Hutchens, a mental health therapist with The Highlands, said.

Hutchens said having this event annually allows for that outreach to occur. Survivors, friends and family of survivors, or anyone looking to support others come to this event to find that community and outreach. This support is an important part of the healing process, she added.

Hutchens also said she hopes this event will help build supportive connections and make people feel as though they have a safe place they can go.

Carter agreed, adding how the music and art aspect of the event aids with the healing process along with finding support.

R-VOICE has been supporting the Penn State community for 40 years. Sophia Mills explained how the partnership with Centre Safe and hosting this event has been going on for years as well.

“It’s evolved to support survivors of interpersonal violence, and we added in the art therapy component to really showcase how important and effective art therapy can be for part of your healing journey,” Mills, the associate director of the R-VOICE, said.

Chloe Crabb, who works alongside Mills, said that collective art can be transformative and powerful for a community such as this.

“My favorite part of this event is the community building aspect,” Crabb, a program coordinator for outreach and prevention at R-VOICE, said.

The pair worked together with Centre Safe to create a supportive environment and community every year in order to see progress and healing within the community.

“Healing happens together,” Mills said. “These incidents are not isolated so really coming together for events like this is incredibly important.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: 'Sexual violence impacts all of us' | R-VOICE holds annual Men Against Violence Walk
author: Paloma Munoz | The Daily Collegian
description:
Content warning: This story discusses sexual and domestic violence. Resources and support are available through Penn State’s Counseling and Psychological Services.

The 2026 Men Against Violence Walk began on the Old Main Lawn on Wednesday, an effort to bring together men and allies to demonstrate their commitment against sexual violence.

The walk was held as part of the Relationship Violence Outreach, Intervention and Community Education Center’s (R-VOICE) Sexual Assault Awareness and Prevention Month events.

“I think for a really long time, the narrative around the world that men play in sexual assault awareness has really been a backseat role in supporting and attending and encouraging events,” Jacob Frank, the organizer of the event and the program coordinator at R-VOICE, said. “I think that it’s time that we make it known that we’re not okay with it.”

Frank said sexual violence is something that “impacts all of us.”

“We all have a role to play in creating a culture of respect and accountability so that we all have a fun and good time,” Frank said.

R-VOICE has been hosting events like this throughout April to bring awareness to sexual assault and the impact it can have on the student body. This event, done in collaboration with Penn State’s Interfraternity Council (IFC), helps show that it’s not just about survivors and victims, but everyone.

Dominic Russo, the administrative vice president of the IFC, said the organization is the second largest student group on campus. Therefore, the group wanted to partner with R-VOICE to show that, even though it’s a male dominated organization, the men understand there is sexual violence happening in the community and that “we can all be better.”

“We could be great people and take a stand against it to make everyone feel safe in the Penn State community,” Russo, a third-year studying telecommunications management, said.

Around 60 to 70 people walked the mile around campus in solidarity with sexual violence survivors. Many chatted while walking, taking turns holding signs and discussing what more they could do regarding the matter.

Cater Marshall, treasurer of Alpha Zeta Fraternity, said the group is “different” and wants to show that.

“We want to represent the brotherhood, and it’s good to see outside faces standing for the same morals and values,” Marshall, a third-year studying turfgrass science, said.

Russo said he’s had friends who are sexual violence survivors and even ones who are uncomfortable to walking around campus at night.

“It’s something very deeply personal to me that I don’t understand at all, and I will never understand as a man,” Russo said. “But, being able to play a part in making the Penn State community safer, one walk at a time, one day at a time, is something that I’m very passionate about.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: 4 Penn State men’s lacrosse players take home All-Big Ten honors
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Four Penn State players took home All-Big Ten accolades to close out the regular season.

Defenseman Alex Ross continued to build steam, taking home All-Big Ten First Team honors for the first time in his collegiate career, having previously been named to the second team for the past two seasons. Not only was he recognized by the college lacrosse world, but the PLL as well, as he was selected by the New York Atlas in the first round of the 2026 PLL College Draft.

Ross, a senior from Davidsonville, Maryland, leads the Nittany Lions with 20 caused turnovers and added 27 ground balls through 12 games. He contributed a goal and an assist on offense as well, presenting a dual threat when he had to run the ball for a clearance.

Short-stick defensive midfielder Jon King earned All-Big Ten Second Team recognition, the first time receiving conference honors in his career. Like Ross, King’s senior season has stood out, with 27 ground balls and 15 caused turnovers.

A pair of freshmen were named to the All-Big Ten Freshman Team, including attackman Jack Iannantuono and goaltender Preston Hawkins.

Iannantuono, recently named the Big Ten Freshman of the Year, leads the Nittany Lions with 32 points through 12 games, including a team-high 16 assists in his true freshman campaign.

Hawkins, after starting the season off on the wrong foot, being pulled midway through his collegiate debut, snowballed momentum throughout the season. He was crucial to winning key games throughout the season, including victories over Princeton, Cornell and Maryland.

The Powell, Ohio, native closed out the regular season with a .508 save percentage and a 9.02 goals-against average, posting a 6-5 regular season record.

title: Penn State men's lacrosse's Jack Iannantuono named Big Ten Freshman of the Year
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the second consecutive year, Penn State has the top freshman in the Big Ten.

Attackman Jack Iannantuono was named the 2026 Big Ten Freshman of the Year on Wednesday, after a stellar start to his collegiate lacrosse career in Happy Valley.

Buy stock now. pic.twitter.com/UKfvZDGP0m— Penn State Men’s Lacrosse (@PennStateMLAX) April 22, 2026

Through 12 games in his true freshman campaign, the Phoenix, Maryland, native paced Penn State with 32 points split between 16 goals and a team-high 16 assists. He tallied a career-high six points against Yale on Feb. 28, scoring two goals and adding four helpers.

He played a key role in helping the blue and white secure a first-round bye in the upcoming Big Ten Tournament, putting up a three-point night against Rutgers in the final game of the regular season to clinch a share at the Big Ten Championship.

He joined Hunter Aquino as the second Nittany Lion in two years to take home Freshman of the Year honors from the conference.

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse falls to Michigan in Big Ten Tournament opener
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s first season under coach Kayla Treanor earned another big milestone as it clinched a Big Ten Tournament spot.

The fifth-seeded Nittany Lions traveled to Ann Arbor to face No. 4 Michigan in the Big Ten quarterfinals, repeating the outcome from the regular season as they fell 13-12.

The blue and white started the game with a slightly different starting lineup than normal, giving sophomore Maggie Rezza and Payton Wainman — who’s second in goals scored on the team — the starting nod.

While Michigan’s offense won the draw control and put a shot on goal, a save by Sydney Manning sent things going the other way.

Less than three minutes into the contest, Penn State took the first lead with graduate midfielder Anna Salerno’s 21st goal of the season.

After four saves by Manning through the opening minutes and Elizabeth Johnson’s first save of the day for Michigan against Rocquette Allen, the Wolverines tied things up with a goal by Emma Bradbury.

Despite strong goalkeeping by Manning, Michigan had nine shots early on, with its second goal being scored by Callie Norris.

Following a man-up advantage with a free position attempt by Erika Ho, the Nittany Lions continued to trail. Less than a minute later, Delaney Radin booked her 54th assist of the year as she dished the ball to Payton Wainman for her 45th goal to tie the game.

While the Nittany Lions earned a pair of draw controls, a turnover gave the ball back to the Wolverines, who re-took the lead with Norris’ second goal of the game.

After a steal by Manning halted Michigan’s scoring attempt, Penn State took the ball back up the field on offense and capitalized with Allen’s first goal of the game, tying things at 3-3.

Coming out of the quarter break, the Nittany Lions earned a quick man-up opportunity after a green card on Michigan. While the card ultimately expired before Penn State scored, Allen netted her second goal of the game 10 seconds after the Wolverines returned to full strength.

Michigan continued to dominate the draw controls, earning its sixth straight control. Norris took advantage of the opportunity, earning a hat trick as she tied things up.

Things continued to be a back-and-forth affair in Ann Arbor as senior Maddie Greco scored her first of the game following a missed free-position attempt by Wainman. That lead didn’t last long, however, as Bradbury scored her second of the day on a fast-break opportunity.

Both defenses stepped up midway through the second quarter, going over nearly without any goals to end the half tied at 5-5.

Coming out of the half, both teams had a chance at scoring, warranting saves by both sides. Four minutes into the half, Ho scored her first goal of the game to take the lead after nearly 15 minutes of silence.

The momentum continued to swing back and forth as the Wolverines tied things back up, as Bradbury dished out her free position attempt to Norris. That success continued into a second straight free position attempt as midfielder Ava Class took the lead for the Wolverines with her first goal of the game.

Bradbury earned her hat trick on the day as Michigan scored three straight goals, taking the biggest lead of the day halfway through the quarter.

With less than a minute left in the third quarter, Ho scored her second goal of the day, this time off a free position opportunity, cutting the deficit to one goal.

Despite bringing things close to the end of the quarter, Norris quickly re-extended Michigan’s lead, scoring her fifth goal of the game 23 seconds into the final quarter. Less than 30 seconds later, the Wolverines scored their second goal in the opening minute of the quarter as Ceci Stein scored her first of the day.

That lead continued to grow with Class’s second goal of the day, marking three unanswered goals from Michigan.

However, Allen stopped the run by the Wolverines as she scored her third of the day, marking her seventh hat trick of the year. Emma Kelly scored less than a minute later, cutting the deficit in half.

Fifty seconds later, Kelly scored her second of the day, marking just her third multi-goal game this season.

With a one-goal game, coach Kayla Treanor switched out Manning for freshman Makenna Gagliardi in net, quickly resulting in a free-position goal by Bradbury. However, Ho quickly struck back with her third goal of the game, prompting a goalie change from the Wolverines.

Michigan continued to have success with free-position attempts, retaking a two-goal lead with Katharine Merrifield’s first goal of the game.

Following two goals on Gagliardi, Manning went back in the net for the Nittany Lions, while Madeline Jensen stayed in net for the Wolverines. With just under four minutes in the contest, Allen scored her fourth goal of the game to make it a one-goal game.

The Nittany Lions took control of the ball with under two minutes to play, but a turnover by Allen gave the Wolverines the ball, winding the clock down for the win.

Up next

The Nittany Lions will await the results of the NCAA Selection Show on Monday, May 3, for their spot in the NCAA Tournament.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Softball coach Clarisa Crowell talks surprising players, end of season approach
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
As the month of May creeps around the corner, Penn State is down to six games left of its regular season. The Nittany Lions travel to Illinois this weekend, before hosting No. 4 Nebraska for their season finale.

Coach Clarisa Crowell met with the media on Wednesday to discuss the team's outlook in the final stretch.

Pleasant surprises

As injuries began to creep up on Penn State, Crowell has made some lineup adjustments in the latter half of the season. This called for different players to show improvement and step up throughout the year.

One of these names is junior outfielder Frankee Flesher, who has had a unique journey to be a part of Crowell’s squad, joining the softball team this season after medically retiring from the soccer team.

“She's really just played solid defense for us, but she's also put together some quality at-bats for us, that's what we always focus on,” Crowell said. “She played in the fall, but she’s never played in a college game, so yeah, she's been a pleasant surprise for sure.”

Another name that stands out for Crowell is freshman outfielder Breanna Hanik, who came alive in conference play. Hanik had been a consistent starter before Big Ten competition began, but was batting near the bottom of the lineup.

The Oak Lawn, Illinois, native posts a .333 batting average in conference play, resulting in a consistent spot as the leadoff hitter.

“I think Bree Hanik has done a really good job. Early on this season, she was hitting lower in the lineup, and then obviously, we've moved her up,” Crowell said. “She's one of our best hitters in conference as well, behind Brooke Klosowicz and Allison Oneacre.”

Finishing strong

Crowell referred to closing out games as the team's ‘Achilles heel’ this season. The Nittany Lions have lost seven games in the seventh inning this season, which is almost half of their 17 total losses.

“Find a way to win five of those games this season, it just looks different,” Crowell said. “But I think in any sport, that’s the hardest thing to do, is to finish strong. I think in order for a team to finish strong, you have to be the tougher team and you've got to have the playmakers that make the plays late.”

In Penn State’s most recent series, they dropped two games to Ohio State, one of which the Nittany Lions led for the entire game.

In the series opener, the blue and white fell 4-3 on a walk-off, but led 3-0 after the first inning. Additionally, the Buckeyes ' game-winning two-run single came with two outs in the frame.

“Every series you go into, you want to put yourself in a situation where you can win the series, and we did that and really we should have finished it and won that first game,” Crowell said. “It's been tough from an emotional standpoint, because we know that our record is not indicative of how good of a team we are, but I think they've handled it the best that they can.”

Nearing the end

With just two series left in the regular season, Penn State is still vying to lock in a spot for the Big Ten Tournament. The tournament welcomes 12 teams, and with an 8-10 conference record, the Nittany Lions currently place 10th.

The blue and white’s upcoming trip to Illinois is a crucial one, to say the least.

“This weekend against Illinois, it's probably the biggest series that we have all year, just in light of what it means, because as we're trying to fight our way into that tournament, and our kids know what we need to do,” Crowell said. “We have got to go 1-0 three times, like there's no option.”

As a few critical games are approaching, Crowell’s biggest message to her team is that the end is near and to finish the season strong.

“My biggest message to them is, all of us, even if you're going to win the championship, we're all near the end, and for me, that's always a tough thing, especially when you love your kids as much as we do,” Crowell said. “The softball piece and winning is obviously a big part of it, but I want them to enjoy every moment that we have left.”

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘I feel honored’ | Penn State’s newcomers set to debut for fans at Beaver Stadium in Blue-White Practice
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s roster looked vastly different the last time fans attended a game at Beaver Stadium.

When Matt Campbell was hired to be the new head coach, he brought much of Iowa State’s core to Happy Valley, none of which had experienced playing in a venue that seats 107,000. The Cyclones’ home stadium, Jack Trice, has a max capacity of 61,500.

But before the new season starts, the Penn State faithful are ready to welcome the fresh faces to their new home with the Blue-White Practice this weekend. To prepare for the environment, the Nittany Lions held their first practice inside Beaver Stadium last Saturday with empty stands.

“Man, it was lit. Beaver is a big stadium — I've never played in a stadium like that,” defensive end Ike Ezeogu said on Tuesday. “Besides, I guess (Arrowhead Stadium) we played in one time. But other than that, Beaver was an amazing experience, and I just can't wait to see all of the 100,000 people in there.”

The Blue-White Practice is a variation of previous years’ Blue-White Game, where the Nittany Lions split up into two teams and played a full scrimmage with additional padding on their helmets to mitigate potential injuries.

Campbell announced that the structure of the event will look different, functioning as a standard practice for the opening 20-25 minutes before transitioning into the familiar spring game feel where the offense and defense will run drives down the field. Therefore, the bones of the event remain largely the same, giving fans a closer look at what the Nittany Lions will be like in the fall.

“One of the neat things for our kids this time around is they're going to get to experience what this fanbase feels like and how special this place really is,” Campbell said. “Even last Saturday, there were a lot of big eyes walking into that stadium and how powerful it feels to walk in there. I think the opportunity to do that with our fan base this weekend — we can't waste a great practice.”

For projected starting wide receiver Chase Sowell, a Beaver Stadium debut is something that he didn’t think was in the cards. A college football journeyman, Sowell played at Colorado, East Carolina and Iowa State before joining many of his teammates at Penn State.

“I can't wait. I've never even thought I'd be playing (at Beaver Stadium),” Sowell said. “I've played at a lot of places, but I've never played at a stadium that looks like this. And I feel like that’s my home. Now, that’s something that I’ve got to play for and represent.

Sowell also spoke about the implications of being not only a player, but a member of the Penn State community. Wearing the blue and white is a representation of the university and its people, both students, locals and football fans, and Sowell noted that he “feels honored” to be able to play in the Happy Valley atmosphere.

“You go back in the history of what Penn State is about, and what their M.O. is and I feel like a lot of those same values tie into what our values were already as a team,” Sowell said. “Being a good person, being a good role model, being a good person in the community, and being a good student, all those things. I feel like this place has always been at that standard.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Here’s what coaches Ikaika Malloe, Christian Smith had to say about DL outlook
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s defensive line went through a complete makeover this offseason, as all four starters from 2025 departed to either the NFL draft or the transfer portal.

But it isn’t just the new faces that make the defensive line different — the Nittany Lions’ defensive tackles are much bigger. That’s something D’Anton Lynn has preferred across his career, and defensive line coach Ikaika Malloe happens to be good at developing those types of players.

As spring practice comes to an end, Malloe, Matt Campbell and edge rushers coach Christian Smith have a better outlook of how the defensive line is going to shape up this season.

“Spring's been really good so far,” Smith said. “First couple days it's like, ‘Oh, we got a lot of work to do.’ But as we go into this last week, you see a lot of growth collectively as a group. And then some individuals are starting to separate themselves a little bit.”

The defensive line group is quite youthful outside of a few UCLA defensive tackle transfers and Iowa State transfer Ike Ezeogu. With that naturally comes some mistakes, and Malloe said “we’re really elementary in terms of our knowledge of the game.”

Malloe wants his group at “level 400” in order for them to be elite and so Lynn can “use them the way he wants to.” In order to reach that threshold, Malloe has been leaning on veterans Siale Taupaki and Keanu Williams.

“It helps that I have two guys that came with me from UCLA, so they're running extra meetings, when I'm not there,” Malloe said. “Those guys can meet with that, and then they, our guys right now, do a really good job coming in the morning. They know I'm a morning guy. So a lot of those guys come in around 5:30-6 o'clock, so I can watch with them. But it's really using their own tools for their own advantage, right?”

The defensive end room specifically is littered with young talent. Yvan Kemajou, Alex McPherson and Max Granville are set to see ample playing time this season alongside Ezeogu, and all four have been impressive this spring.

Smith highlighted McPherson, Kemajou and Ezeogu specifically as players who have stood out to him while Granville is sidelined with an injury.

“They've done a good job of understanding the playbook, understanding what they're supposed to do. And then now they're getting to the point where they can start the formation and see and recognize things, and really have trusted the technique that they're playing with and gotten better,” Smith said. “I think as they continue to go and master their craft they'll be really, really good players in the Big Ten.”

Because the defensive end room is younger, it doesn’t have a vocal leader just yet. Smith is looking for someone to step up, though, as they’re currently relying on guys like Taupaki and Williams to lead the entire defensive line group as spring ball nears its end.

“I think the veteran leadership is in the interior right now, in our room, so we got to continue to find more guys in the defensive end room that can be (leaders) because we do break up at times,” Smith said. “Who's going to be the voice of the position group, if we're doing individual d-ends, and we're not having the energy that we need to have, but it means the world to have those big guys in the middle.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Sustainability State College hosts Earth and Arbor Day Festival
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Sustainability State College held an Earth and Arbor Day Festival as part of its third annual Earth Day celebration from 5 to 8 p.m. on Tuesday at Sidney Friedman Park.

Organizations like Penn State Sustainability, Women in Nuclear (WIN) at Penn State, Centre Region Pennsylvania Senior Environmental Corps and Schlow Centre Region Library set up tables to participate in the event. There were also performances from musicians like Sean Thomas, Ham on a Stick and Code Blue.

Sarah Landauer, who tabled for the Sustainable Food Systems Network (SFSN), said she saw the festival as a good way to introduce the community to sustainability resources in the borough.

“It's great for the, one, community to come out and learn and, two, for community organizations to feel supported by other organizations in the community,” Landauer said.

SFSN’s table was handing out seeds for people to create their own gardens, which Landauer said is a fun way for people to get excited about sustainability.

“Producing your own food can be really educational, fun, rewarding and it can also be a great way to kind of work with the environment and understand how to take care of the soil, take care of the earth in a way that will give back to you,” Landauer said. “It's like a reciprocal relationship.”

At 6 p.m., State College Borough Council Member Matt Herndon presented two mayoral proclamations declaring April 22 as Earth Day and April 254 as Arbor Day in State College.

Before reading the proclamations, Herndon said in an interview that he saw the festival as a way to inspire the community to take action to combat climate change.

“It's something that we're living with right now as we see wildfires, as we see increased storm activity, as we see rising sea levels, as we see more ticks in our area,” Herndon said. “These are all results of climate change and they are getting worse and will continue to get worse until we stop our emissions and deal with the actual root of the problem.”

He said organizations like CentreBike and efforts to expand safe cycling in the borough shows there are many practical ways available for people to begin making more sustainable choices.

“I think the thing that keeps most people off bikes, it's not that they are not capable of it, it's that they're afraid of cars injuring or killing them,” Herndon said. “Having separated bikeways, I think, is a very strong action that we can take to help people be more comfortable.”

Molly MacRae, who was invited to the festival by a friend, said she was amazed by the diversity of organizations present at the festival.

“I feel like, typically, when you go to something like this, it's the same thing over and over and they have one pamphlet with the same kind of information,” MacRae, a second-year studying environmental resource management, said. “It's kind of neat how each one has a craft or an activity rather than having to pick it out of a pamphlet and everyone tabling seems really about it, very knowledgeable and wanting to share what their organization's about.”

While she said she appreciated the festival as a means for community outreach and education, the borough could do more to further its sustainability efforts.

“I think that a lot of our green spaces aren't really put to their best use,” MacRae said. “I think a lot of them could be more home to native plants rather than mowed grass that our taxes are paying to mow instead of the cultivation of native species. I think something like this, though, does do a lot to educate the general public and the members of the borough.”

title: Penn State football draft profile | Safety Zakee Wheatley showcases size, play-making abilities
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Less than a minute into Penn State’s first road trip without James Franklin, safety Zakee Wheatley made a momentum-shifting play. The ball popped into the air, and Wheatley snagged it with one hand to cut Iowa’s opening possession short.

It was another nifty snag for the Nittany Lions turnover king, further cementing his athleticism and turnover threat as a safety.

The redshirt senior made the most of his five years in Happy Valley, taking a starting role in his last two seasons. Wheatley finished as one of eight players in his class to spend their entire five-year career at Penn State, showcasing his commitment to the program.

Wheatley had his chance in the spotlight last season after Kevin Winston Jr. and Jaylen Reed departed to the NFL, and he made the most of it with 74 tackles, two tackles for loss and two pass deflections in 2025.

“It’s a blessing to come back to Penn State,” Wheatley said at Penn State Pro Day. “I feel satisfied with it, I put it all on the line. I trained a lot for this.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-3

Weight: 202 pounds

40-yard dash: 4.62 seconds

Vertical jump: 33 inches

Broad jump: 10-foot-4

Pro shuttle: 4.11 seconds

3-cone drill: 6.89 seconds

Projections

Projected round drafted: Rounds 3-4

Player comparison: Miles Killebrew

Scouting report

During his true freshman season, Wheatley played cornerback, recording a pair of tackles in just four games played. From there, he moved to safety, where he also succeeded and ultimately took a starting role.

Wheatley was the only Nittany Lion to participate in both the NFL Scouting Combine and Penn State Pro Day, seeing it as another opportunity to put up better numbers.

At the combine, he participated in both the broad and vertical jump, finishing third-last and last against other safeties. At Penn State, his 40-yard dash would’ve also finished last among safeties at the combine.

Wheatley makes up for his lack of elite speed in other areas. He possesses a closing burst that contributes to his tackling success and exudes body control in every area of the game.

Franklin said after the Fiesta Bowl in 2024, where Wheatley won defensive player of the game, that the safety could play wide receiver if he wanted to, showcasing his versatility and catch point skills.

In the Orange Bowl, it was his physicality that allowed him to pick off Notre Dame’s Riley Leonard to swing the momentum early in Penn State’s favor, showcasing his ability to make plays in big moments.

In 2025, Wheatley was the most consistent player amidst an inconsistent defense, helping him stand out despite a decrease in interceptions and pass deflections. He is a solid tackler, but could use some improvement with finishing.

Best team fit: Pittsburgh Steelers

While the Pittsburgh Steelers have a strong defensive front with stars Cameron Heyward and T.J. Watt, it’s recently struggled with depth in the secondary.

There’s a lot of unknown at safety for the Steelers this year following the departure of Chuck Clark and Kyle Dugger. DeShon Elliott will be looking for a role in the starting lineup after a season-ending injury in October, and former Nittany Lion Jaquan Brisker will look to be an impact defender in his first year in Pittsburgh.

Wheatley spent his true freshman year with Brisker and could look to suit up beside him again with the Steelers. He could also join former Nittany Lion Joey Porter Jr. in the secondary.

The Maryland native has room to grow before taking on a starting position in the big leagues, but would have the opportunity to learn from a previous mentor at Penn State.

title: Penn State professionals help students prepare for post-graduation career success
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
Many students begin the search for post-graduation jobs during their undergraduate years, hoping to get a jump-start as the workforce landscape grows increasingly competitive.

While the job search can be difficult, resource specialists serve in spaces such as the Bank of America Career Center and Bellisario College of Communications’ internship office to support students looking for early career opportunities.

Recently, the Bank of America Career Services Center launched a program called the Career Readiness Initiative. The program was created for a number of factors, including the COVID-19 pandemic.

“The COVID pandemic caused a lot of recruiters to figure out that they had to recruit students virtually,” Bob Orndorff, the senior director of the Bank of America Career Service Center, said. “It saved a lot of time and money.”

Ordnorff said they hold one hour career counseling sessions, and they encourage repeat sessions with the same student. This allows for a more personalized approach to career counseling. He added that the initiative is helping students deal with transition anxiety as they move from student lives into the professional world.

Having summer jobs and internships is vital to prepare students for the real world, whether the job is related to students’ major or not, according to Kevin Fleck.

Fleck, the director of career services for the College of Earth and Mineral Sciences, said many students come to him saying they’ve never had a job in material sciences, but they’ve had one in different fields.

For example, if a student was a lifeguard in the summer and exemplified their ability to be a hard worker and excel, the experience still stands to “help the student understand where they fit in the work culture,” Fleck said.

“They also think that these experiences do not count, but they most certainly do, and that is why it is important to have them on your resume,” Fleck said.

Fleck said students often experience setbacks in the job search.

“When I was applying for jobs I applied to more than 300 jobs, and rejection is something that definitely happens and it stings almost every time, especially if you had your hopes up that you would fit well with a company,” Fleck said.

Fleck said he tells his students these choices are business decisions and not personal — the job search is all about finding the right fit for them.

“The most common fear is that the person may feel like they do not fit on the team,” Fleck said. “I tell my students all the time that they are not going to know everything on the first day, but what I would do, unless you are filling out HR paperwork, is do not be in your office.”

Fleck said it’s important to get to know your team, go to team meetings and introduce yourself to everybody to establish a connection.

There are three specific communication internship and job fairs to ensure students are marketable and well-prepared, according to Bob Martin, assistant dean for internships and career placement in the Bellisario College of Communications.

Success in the City is one of the fairs the college offers. According to Martin, about six bus loads of students are taken to New York to meet over 70 companies, including Disney, ABC, Edelman and major advertising agencies.

The second event is the Heritage Internship Career Fair (JobExpo.Comm) where companies more regional to Pennsylvania come and speak to students. COMM Careers in the Capital is another internship and job fair that takes place in Washington D.C. early in the fall semester.

Between the three career fairs, there will be almost 175 companies recruiting students. These events are held because companies do “on-time hiring” in the communications industry — meaning recruiters are looking to fill positions within six to eight weeks of its posting, according to Martin.

“It starts with understanding yourself, but also understanding your interests and how your interests can meet the recruiter's needs,” Martin said.

Some of the strengths Martin sees in students comes from the eight core competencies, created by the National Association of Colleges and Employers. The eight skills include career and self development, communication, critical thinking, equity and inclusion, leadership, professionalism, teamwork and technology.

“If you are a public relations major working with a PR (public relations) agency, they will want you to know how to write,” Martin said. “Most positions in communication require you to be a good communicator and writer and some soft skills too.”

Martin says these skills are critical. Bellisario has been working with the Bank of America Career Services Center to help advance students’ career readiness, as well as engage them with the eight core competencies.

“We tell our students to do some self-reflection,” Martin said. “I'm seeing more and more students that don't have a focus of what they want to do.”
