title: Former Penn State women’s basketball freshman Nyla McFadden transfers to UCF
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Despite being an electric scorer for Penn State down the stretch of the season, freshman Nyla McFadden has elected to take her talents elsewhere.

McFadden committed to UCF, according to Talia Goodman of On3.

The 6-foot Fort Lauderdale native started in just eight games during her freshman year, but came alive with five double-digit performances in the second-half of the season.

McFadden will now spend her sophomore year with Gabe Lazzo, who’s entering his first season with the Knights.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘Our team likes to have fun’ | Energetic second inning boosts Penn State softball over Saint Francis
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State returned to Beard Field on Tuesday night, welcoming Saint Francis for both its final nonconference and midweek matchup of the regular season. The Nittany Lions came away with a convincing 12-0 win over the Red Flash

“What was most special about tonight was it was really a team effort, everybody was able to get into the game and contribute in their own way,” coach Clarisa Crowell said. “When you can win a game like that with everybody, that's part of it; it's a pretty special thing.”

The blue and white’s victory was highlighted by a second inning that brought 10 runs across for Penn State and saw contributions from its whole lineup.

“We actually came into this game tonight just saying, you know what, we need to not only hit the long ball or the double, but we need to be able to put bunts down,” Crowell said. “We squeezed a run in, we stole some bases, because we're going to need to be able to do things like that with our games coming up this weekend and next weekend.”

Small ball and steals is what got the second frame momentum started for the Nittany Lions.

The second inning started with a leadoff single from junior Michela Barbanente. After Barbanente stole second base, back-to-back perfectly executed bunts from Frankee Flesher and Madison Seidel allowed the Itasca, Illinois native to come around to score.

Sophomore Natalie Lieto, batting in the eight hole, stepped into the box for the first time of the night. The Monroe, Connecticut native, wasted no time, hitting a two-run home run on the first offering she saw from Saint Francis pitcher Ella Zoss.

The two-run shot from Lieto was just the start of the party for Penn State’s bats.

“We always want to go out, we want to compete hard, we want to play with high energy, regardless of what the scoreboard says, or regardless of the result,” Crowell said. “We can always control how we play the game and our kids did that.”

Following the long ball, junior Brooke Klosowicz knocked an RBI triple, immediately followed by an RBI double from freshman Allison Oneacre.

At this point in the game, the momentum and high energy were felt all throughout the air surrounding Beard Field, but most importantly, there was a groove in the Penn State dugout.

“Giving your energy, even if that's all you can give, it's just, honestly, our team likes to have fun,” Seidel said. “And that's a really good representation of who we are and what our culture is.”

Barbanente stepped up to the plate for the second time of the inning as the Nittany Lions led 5-0 with two outs. The junior kept the slugfest going with a three-run home run.

Following Barbanente, Flesher reached base and Seidel decided that an eight-run lead wasn’t enough. The senior catcher launched a two-run shot, the first of her final campaign in Happy Valley.

“I mean, you're always just working the process and hoping for good outcomes, and it was really awesome. It was really awesome to have a team win,” Seidel said.”A lot of us put home runs up, but it's always special as seniors to do it.”

Aside from the 10 runs, the Nittany Lions finished their high-energy second inning with eight hits and just one runner left on base.

Penn State looks to take this energy and momentum into the home stretch of its season, with just two conference series left against Illinois and Nebraska.

“We want to finish strong. I mean, we have a great group of girls, and we don't want the season to end,” graduate Cara Bohner said. “'We’re going to keep pushing for each other, doing little things right, and trying to make it happen, which I think we will.”

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball utilizes long ball, defeats Bucknell
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
The slumping campaign continued for Penn State over the weekend when the Nittany Lions dropped two of three to Rutgers, but the squad looked to bounce back at home in its third Dollar Dog Night against Bucknell on Tuesday.

Penn State won a back-and-forth contest 9-6 over Bucknell behind a huge offensive game by Michael Anderson, who blasted two homers in the night.

The blue and white took its positive offensive momentum from Sunday’s win into the contest and pounced on the Bison early. It took just the leadoff batter in the opening frame for Penn State to open the scoring.

Left fielder Jayden Davis took left-hander Frankie Bilecki deep to left and put the Nittany Lions ahead 1-0. A few batters later, second baseman Bryce Molinaro knocked a single to right, but third baseman Spencer Barnett grounded into an inning-ending double play.

Meanwhile, right-hander Mason Horwat was dealing on the mound for the blue and white as he retired the first five Bison batters. Bucknell was still held scoreless after the second, but Penn State added to its lead.

Bilecki struggled with his command and walked right fielder Jack Porter on five pitches and then hit first baseman Kevin Karstetter. The pressure continued mounting for the sophomore and he walked catcher Avery Smith to load the bases with no outs.

Center fielder Maddox McDonald dug into the batter’s box with a chance to do some damage. The senior lifted a sacrifice fly to left and plated Porter to extend Penn State’s lead 2-0. However, Bilecki stopped the bleeding and retired the next two Nittany Lions.

In a two-run hole early, Bucknell threatened in the third with a couple of base runners. Horwat recorded his second strikeout for the first out, but Deacon Bowne lined a one-out single up the middle.

Billy Fluharty grounded to Molinaro at third, but the Nittany Lions couldn’t turn two as he beat out the throw to first. With two outs, Michael Trommer roped a single back up the middle and Horwat faced two runners on.

Yet the junior settled in and struck out the next Bison to end the threat. While Bucknell still couldn’t get on the scoreboard, Penn State extended its lead once again in the bottom of the third.

Bilecki’s command continued to struggle and he hit designated hitter Anderson. Barnett followed a few batters later with a one-out single through the right side. The Nittany Lions continued the small ball as Porter knocked a RBI single through the left side and Penn State’s lead grew to 3-0.

After the Bison escaped the third frame without any more damage than just the one run, its bats came alive and plated a run. Coach Mike Gambino turned to right-hander Frankie Sanchez for relief in the fourth and Bucknell responded well to the change.

Zach Selfon peppered a one-out single up the middle and John Calabrese smashed a double into right and Sanchez was quickly in a jam. Ryan Facinelli grounded to third, but Selfon scored and the Bison cut into the lead, 3-1. Sanchez recorded a pivotal strikeout for the third out and kept Penn State in front by two runs.

While Bucknell brought the game closer, the Nittany Lions quickly responded in their half of the fourth. The Bison brought in left-hander Mikey Myro, but the blue and white quickly figured him out.

After two groundouts, Davis walked again and gave Anderson an opportunity to rake with two outs. The team’s homerun leader powered his 17th home run of the campaign to left and extended Penn State’s lead 5-1.

While the Nittany Lions were comfortably up by four runs, Bucknell exploded in the fifth and scored five runs. Gambino utilized three different pitchers in the frame as the Bison had an answer for each arm it faced.

Robert Brown III, Matthew VanOstenbridge and Dimond Loosli all threw in the lopsided inning. Bucknell drew four walks, totaled two hits and capitalized off the two times a Penn State pitcher hit a batter. Ty Lagoni, Garrett Mathias, Facinelli and Andrew Swenson all recorded an RBI in the treacherous frame.

After the Bison suddenly took a 6-5 lead, the Nittany Lions swiftly answered later in the fifth with a three-spot. Porter, Karstetter and Smith all totaled hits in the frame with Smith and McDonald recording the RBIs to give Penn State the lead back, 8-6.

Both squads were held quiet in the later innings until the eighth when Anderson blasted his second homer of the game over the center field and provided the blue and white with a pivotal insurance run, 9-6.

Ben DeMell recorded the save for the blue and white in the final frame, securing its second straight victory.

Up Next

Penn State travels cross-country and faces No. 19 Oregon for a three-game series. The first game is slated at 9:05 p.m. Friday and will be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball earns dominant run-rule victory over Saint Francis
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State took its first road series to Ohio State for a Big Ten weekend, falling short in two games in Columbus. The Nittany Lions played their last nonconference opponent, Saint Francis, in a midweek matchup.

The blue and white completed their 14th run-rule victory of the season, defeating Saint Francis 12-0 in five innings

Junior righty Bridget Nemeth got things going in the circle, while junior shortstop Kaylie Walters made her return to the field after a midseason injury.

A quick inning, as Nemeth was able to secure her first strikeout of the game.

Nothing on the scoreboard for Penn State in the bottom of the first, but three batters had deep hits to right field, yet nothing over the fence.

The Glendora, California native got two more strikeouts, keeping the scoreboard clean going into the bottom of the second.

Junior infielder Michela Barbanente got things started for the blue and white with a single up the middle. Barbanente was able to sneak past the Red Flash defense to steal second. Junior outfielder Frankee Flesher was able to nail a bunt to get first base.

A sacrifice bunt from junior catcher Madison Seidel got Barbanente home as Flesher got to third.

Sophomore Natalie Lieto, on her first pitch, homered to center field, getting her 11th of the year.

Saint Francis gave freshman lefty Ella Moss the start in the circle. Moss walked Walters. Junior Brooke Klosowicz was able to bring Walter home with a triple to center.

Freshman Allison Oneacre looked to go over the fence but was short; she was able to secure a double as Klosowicz scored the fifth run of the game.

With two outs, freshman Gabby Gradishar was able to draw a walk. With two Nittany Lions in scoring position, Barbanente stepped into her second at-bat of the game and hit a homer to right field.

The Red Flash pitching staff made a change, giving freshman right-hander Zoe Kesterson a chance to get out of the mess.

Flesher got a single to left field. Then Seidel got her first home run of the season in the bottom of the second. Keterson gave up her first walk, but a groundout ended the ten-run inning.

Penn State led 10-0 going into the third frame.

Freshman righty Mackenzie Duncan entered the circle, while senior catcher Jordyn Jochims and Cara Bohner entered the game to start the third.

Duncan was able to keep a clean scoreboard, securing two strikeouts.

Sophomore Macy Chamberlin walked. Freshman Kali Kawowski made an appearance at the plate but was unsuccessful as she hit a flyout. Junior Jordan George also stepped into the batter's box but was stuck out, ending the third.

The Nittany Lions led 10-0 going into the fourth.

Coach Clarisa Crowell made changes in the field to start the fourth. Moving Jordan to first and Karwowski to third.

Duncan got a three-up, three-down inning, striking out all three Saint Francis batters.

Barbantene started things out as she looked to hit one out of the park, but as the ball was dropped into right field, the Red Flash defense was able to get the first out.

Flesher walked and stole a base. Bohner homered to right field, making the fourth of the game and her first in Happy Valley.

Penn State headed into the top of the fifth up 12-0.

Sophomore righty Abigail Britton entered the circle and closed out the game in the fifth.

Up next

Penn State continues action with the rest of its Big Ten slate, heading to face Illinois on the road. The first game of the three-game series is slated for Friday with a first pitch time of 6 p.m.

title: Penn State men’s lacrosse’s Andrew Beard earns Big Ten Freshman of the Week
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
A Nittany Lion ended the regular season with an award.

Penn State’s Andrew Beard was named the Big Ten Freshman of the Week after his standout performance in the blue and white’s 16-8 victory over Rutgers on Friday night.

Finishing the regular season with honors. pic.twitter.com/snr4LnxhDO— Penn State Men’s Lacrosse (@PennStateMLAX) April 21, 2026

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: ‘I love Penn State’ | DE Max Granville’s unique year nears end with return to field
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Max Granville has had one of the more unique careers as a Penn State player thus far, entering his third season in Happy Valley.

Granville reclassified and joined the team in what should’ve been his senior year of high school. Before the following season, he suffered a season-ending injury in May after expectations were raised ahead of his redshirt freshman season.

“The way I was raised,” Granville said. “I think my parents did a great job making sure I wasn't soft. My dad was always hard on me. So I feel like I'm able to handle situations like that.”

While recovering from his injury last season, Granville was often animated on the sidelines, showing support for his teammates. The defensive end learned not only about himself, but also about his play on the field while recovering.

“I learned a lot of different things, even about the game,” Granville said. “I feel like I have a better feel for the game, even though I wasn't out there playing, just understanding how to read tackles on certain things and then also giving me confidence, like saying, ‘Man, I could have made that play.’ I feel like it showed me, I do feel like I'm a special kind of player, and I can make big plays when my team needs me.”

Granville’s recovery process lasted through the coaching staff change, but he had the opportunity to transfer in the offseason. The redshirt sophomore knew his heart was still in Happy Valley, however, which made his decision even easier when the Nittany Lions hired defensive coordinator D’Anton Lynn.

“After thinking through some things, I just really love Penn State, and after talking to coach Lynn,” Granville said. “I will say too, in the week in New York against Clemson, it really just had me thinking, ‘Man, I love Penn State and I don't want this to be my last game.’”

Granville is still working back from his season-ending injury last season, while also recovering from a “minor back issue.” While out, Granville has been working off to the side, getting in reps and working on adjustments in his run defense with the changing size of his interior teammates.

Despite not being able to work with his teammates on the field much, Granville is still making an impact and an influence on the line. He’s helped bridge the gap between returning Penn State players and transfers, but has also shown glimpses of what he could do on the field.

“As a player, I haven't really seen much, obviously, because he's hurt, but I think that he's going to be a great player,” defensive end Ikenna Ezeogu said.

With the recent history of pass rushers having success at Penn State and going to the NFL, Granville sees a lot of his game in some of those stars, as he attempts to etch his name next to the greats to put on the uniform.

The redshirt sophomore is expected to be back full-time in the summer, which will allow him to be full-go for fall camp leading up to the season. In the meantime, Granville’s working on off-the-field preparations that’ll help him find success on the field.

“Watching film on guys who I feel like have similar play styles to me like Micah (Parsons), Abdul (Carter), Chop (Robisnon), learning how they get off the ball,” Granville said. “Then just athleticism, different things like ankle mobility, hip mobility, making sure I'm flexible enough to bend the corner and run game.”

title: Penn State men’s volleyball lands 7 on All-EIVA teams
author: Liana Tiamzon | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State continued its dominant 2026 campaign with seven players earning All-EIVA honors, the conference announced Monday.

SEVEN All-EIVA 🦁🦁🦁🦁🦁🦁🦁𝟭𝘀𝘁 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗺 - Sean Harvey𝟭𝘀𝘁 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗺 - Ryan Merk𝟭𝘀𝘁 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗺 - Owen Rose𝟮𝗻𝗱 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗺 - Gaige Gabriel𝟮𝗻𝗱 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗺 - Matthew Luoma𝟮𝗻𝗱 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗺 - Michael Schwob𝗛𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗠𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 - Tristan Hassell#WeAre pic.twitter.com/IHdEH0wZsB— Penn State Men's Volleyball (@PennStateMVBALL) April 21, 2026

Leading the way for the Nittany Lions was Sean Harvey, who was honored as the EIVA Player of the Year, and Ryan Merk and Owen Rose, who were all named to the All-EIVA First Team. The trio has been instrumental in Penn State’s success this season, helping power the team to an undefeated 12-0 conference record.

Harvey has emerged as Penn State’s primary offensive weapon, consistently leading in kills while maintaining an efficient hitting percentage.

Merk anchored the back line as the team’s libero, providing stability in serve-receive and defense. Rose made his mark at the net as one of the top blockers in the conference. In his final series, he averaged 1.80 blocks per set and leads the EIVA in blocks with 129 on the season, sitting at 460 blocks in his career.

The Nittany Lions’ depth was further recognized with three All-EIVA Second-Team selections in Gaige Gabriel, Matthew Luoma and Micheal Schwob. Each has played a key role in the Nittany Lions’ balanced lineup, contributing on both ends of the court throughout conference play.

Tristan Hassell rounded out the group, earning All-EIVA Honorable Mention honors after delivering multiple standout performances in his freshman season.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State women’s basketball freshman Viktoria Ranisavljevic transfers to UAB
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After just one year in Happy Valley, Viktoria Ranisavljevic is off to a new home.

Ranisavljevic committed to UAB, she announced on Tuesday on Instagram.

The Swiss guard played a smaller role during her freshman season, making just nine starts through her 26 games played.

Ranisavljevic averaged just 4.6 points per game, with one double-digit outing coming against Cincinnati in the second game of the year.

Ranisavljevic will now don the green and gold as she suits up for the Blazers, who came off an 11-19 record last year.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball outside hitter Sean Harvey named EIVA Player of the Year
author: Liana Tiamzon | For the Collegian
description:
Sean Harvey’s breakout sophomore season reached another milestone as he was named the EIVA Player of the Year, becoming the 24th player in Penn State program history to earn the honor.

𝙀𝙄𝙑𝘼 𝙋𝙇𝘼𝙔𝙀𝙍 𝙊𝙁 𝙏𝙃𝙀 𝙔𝙀𝘼𝙍 🏆🦁 24th in program history🦁 EIVA leader in hitting percentage🦁 21 matches with double-digit kills#WeAre pic.twitter.com/DCNf8WtzM0— Penn State Men's Volleyball (@PennStateMVBALL) April 21, 2026

The sophomore has been a driving force behind Penn State’s offense all season, establishing himself as one of the most efficient and reliable attackers in the conference. Harvey led the EIVA in hitting percentage while consistently producing at a high volume, recording double-digit kills in 21 matches.

Serving as the Nittany Lions’ primary offensive weapon, Harvey also led the team by a wide margin in total kills and played a key role in Penn State’s perfect 12-0 conference record.

His efficiency and consistency have been defined by his season hitting percentage of .372, while averaging 3.80 kills per set, anchoring an offense that has relied on him in both high-pressure moments and extended matches.

The player of the year recognition adds to an already decorated season for Harvey, who earned four EIVA Offensive Player of the Week honors throughout the year.

As Penn State heads into postseason play, Harvey’s dominance will remain central to the Nittany Lions’ championship push.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball coach Mark Pavlik named EIVA Coach of the Year Honors in final season
author: Liana Tiamzon | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State coach Mark Pavlik was named the EIVA Coach of the Year after leading the Nittany Lions to a dominant 2026 regular season.

𝟭𝟭𝘅 𝙀𝙄𝙑𝘼 𝘾𝙊𝘼𝘾𝙃 𝙊𝙁 𝙏𝙃𝙀 𝙔𝙀𝘼𝙍 🏆🦁 EIVA regular season champs🦁 Unbeaten in EIVA (12-0)🦁 26th season with 20+ wins#WeAre pic.twitter.com/shD6XMGBZW— Penn State Men's Volleyball (@PennStateMVBALL) April 21, 2026

In his 32nd season at the helm, Pavlik guided Penn State to a 20-7 overall record and a perfect 12-0 mark in conference play, securing the program’s place atop the EIVA standings. The Nittany Lions also lead the nation in blocking, highlighting one of the most effective defensive units in the country.

The honor adds to an already historic career for Pavlik, who announced that the 2026 season will be his final year leading the program. He currently holds a 694-241 career record, winning over 74% of his matches, and ranks third all-time in career wins among collegiate men’s volleyball coaches.

Under Pavlik's leadership, Penn State has been the standard in the EIVA, winning 25 conference tournament titles and 27 regular season championships. A 10-time EIVA Coach of the Year, he has posted a remarkable 337-28 record in conference play.

On the national stage, Pavlik led the Nittany Lions to a 2008 NCAA championship and 25 NCAA Tournament appearances, including four trips to the title match. He has also developed some of the sport's top talent, coaching AVCA National Players of the Year Ivan Contreras and Matt Anderson, along with dozens of All-American and Olympic athletes.

As Penn State heads into postseason play, Pavlik's final season continues to add to a legacy for the Nittany Lions.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘It’s a privilege’ | Penn State WRs coach Kashif Moore working to build relationships in new environment
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Kashif Moore was the last addition to Matt Campbell’s coaching staff after Noah Pauley’s unexpected parting for the NFL — Pauley had come with Campbell from Iowa State and brought numerous transfers with him, such as projected starters Brett Eskildsen and Chase Sowell.

Pauley’s departure left Campbell in a scramble to find a new wide receivers coach late in the cycle in February, so the hiring process was sped up to give Moore the most time possible with his players after he decided to leave Colorado State after just two months.

“It was definitely a unique interview process, I would say, just because it happened so fast,” Moore said. “But it was great because some of the things that he talked about were in alignment with some of my values. I was really excited about that, and then just to get the opportunity to come here. Man, it's a privilege. It's an honor. Man, I'm very thankful.”

Campbell said Moore was the last guy he interviewed for the job, so he clearly liked what he saw in the former UConn wide receivers coach. So far, what Campbell heard during the interview process has matched what he’s seen on the practice field.

He praised Moore’s ability to work with individual players on their specific areas of improvement on Tuesday, taking into consideration the age of the room. He noted that Moore’s guidance will be crucial to the future success of the receiver room.

“His ability to articulate the teaching of the wide receiver fundamentals and the detail that it takes to play for the wide receiver (is great),” Campbell said. “I think that's the biggest thing that you just don't know. But the ability to articulate, what are those key factors, what are those key separation points, the fundamental details that you have to do to be able to play a wide receiver at a really high rate.”

For Moore, he stepped into a brand new room where he quickly had to learn the lay of the land. Building relationships with the younger receivers for lasting growth into the future became his focus considering the youthfulness of the group as Campbell mentioned.

As a result, Moore has prided himself on being a “relationships coach,” establishing a connection with his guys off the field prior to training for the first time with them.

“Coming along a little bit later in the process, I had to accelerate things as quickly as possible,” Moore said. “Just being able to get on the phone right before I even got here with someone who I received and tried to establish a relationship. Day one and I was here bringing guys up to meet with them one on one was very important to me.”

Sowell had an existing relationship with his new coach, as he was recruited by Moore when he was in the process of transferring out of Colorado. Sowell looked at playing under Moore at UConn, but Sowell chose East Carolina over the Huskies.

Moore ended up coaching Sowell eventually, though quite unexpectedly, and that prior relationship has helped Sowell adjust to his new coach.

“I know what he's about,” Sowell said. “I know what he brings, and just what he's done in the game, what level he's played at, who he's played under, who he's played with. Those things have played a big factor in developing the room. So I think he'll bring a good aspect to the room that we didn't have before.”

There’s always pressure for players and coaches to perform on-field at Penn State, but Moore stressed the importance of building an individual outside of the sport. He’s juggling the responsibilities of preparing the current and future generations of Nittany Lion receivers along with teaching his student-athletes how to be the best versions of themselves, part of his “holistic approach” to coaching.

“Obviously, we're here to win games and do something special here with the high expectations and the high standards of excellence here at Penn State,” Moore said. “But the holistic approach, when it comes to the academics, holding guys accountable to everything both on and off the field — we're trying to build future husbands and fathers that are going to be successful people when football is long gone.”

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse earns 5 All-Big Ten honors
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
The first season under coach Kayla Treanor continues to earn its honors and accolades.

Five Nittany Lions earned All-Big Ten honors Tuesday, including two First Team All-Big Ten honorees.

𝐅𝐈𝐑𝐒𝐓 𝐓𝐄𝐀𝐌 𝐀𝐋𝐋-𝐁𝐈𝐆 𝐓𝐄𝐍Delaney is officially one of the best attackers in the conference😤 pic.twitter.com/yXQ6H0L7kh— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) April 21, 2026

After setting the program’s single-season assist record against Rutgers, junior midfielder Delaney Radin was named to First Team All-Big Ten.

Throughout the year, Radin accumulated 53 assists, leading the team, conference and sitting fifth in the nation. The Long Beach, New York, native also recorded 26 goals.

𝐅𝐈𝐑𝐒𝐓 𝐓𝐄𝐀𝐌 𝐀𝐋𝐋-𝐁𝐈𝐆 𝐓𝐄𝐍Maggie earns her first All-Big Ten accolade‼️ pic.twitter.com/r7pbh0lCc9— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) April 21, 2026

Joining Radin on the first team is senior defender Maggie Driver. During her fourth year, she started in 16 games, picking up 24 ground balls with 27 caused turnovers and 26 draw controls.

𝐒𝐄𝐂𝐎𝐍𝐃 𝐓𝐄𝐀𝐌 𝐀𝐋𝐋-𝐁𝐈𝐆 𝐓𝐄𝐍Anna grabs her third All-Conference nods 😤 pic.twitter.com/hYfO1Swvnq— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) April 21, 2026

In her first season with the blue and white, Salerno started 17 games with 20 goals and eight assists.

Rounding out the honorees is a pair of freshmen, Rocquette Allen and Susannah O’Connell, on the All-Freshman Team.

𝐁𝐈𝐆 𝐓𝐄𝐍 𝐀𝐋𝐋-𝐅𝐑𝐄𝐒𝐇𝐌𝐀𝐍 𝐓𝐄𝐀𝐌Rocquette earns a UNANIMOUS selection‼️ pic.twitter.com/yKxnXtS0Dq— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) April 21, 2026

Allen adds another award to her historic season after earning her sixth Big Ten Freshman of the Week honors on Monday. Her All-Freshman Team mark was the only unanimous honor for the blue and white.

The star freshman finished third on the team with 41 goals and third in points with an additional 22 assists. Allen recorded six hat tricks during the year, including four five-goal games.

𝐁𝐈𝐆 𝐓𝐄𝐍 𝐀𝐋𝐋-𝐅𝐑𝐄𝐒𝐇𝐌𝐀𝐍 𝐓𝐄𝐀𝐌Suz is one of the best freshman in the land 😮‍💨 pic.twitter.com/iuMogP4RqR— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) April 21, 2026

Outside of Allen, O’Connell was the only other freshman to start in all 17 games this season. The Summit, New Jersey, native caused 25 turnovers and picked up 23 ground balls in her first year in Happy Valley.

title: Weekly recap | Penn State baseball had back and forth week with some bright spots
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State continued its underwhelming season last week, dropping three of its last four games.

The week started on Wednesday with a neutral-site game against West Virginia in Maryland, which it lost in a low-scoring game. Then the Nittany Lions returned to State College for a three-game conference series against Rutgers. The blue and white dropped the first two games before getting a win in the third.

Penn State will have to try to build on this last win and keep battling to try to compete for a spot in the Big Ten Tournament.

Bi-polar batting

The Nittany Lions were only able to score one run in two of their games this past week. However, in their other two outings, they scored 11 and 14, the latter tying their season high. Weirdly, their best and worst scoring days often come against the same opponents. It first happened against Purdue last month. The first matchup saw a 2-1 loss, then the next day they won 14-9.

You can also see this against Ohio State. Earlier this season, the blue and white won its opening series game against the Buckeyes 10-6. The next day, it lost 3-2, but in the series finale, it matched the score of the first but still lost 15-10. Penn State needs to stay consistent throughout its series. It shows flashes of good ball, then looks like a different team against the same opponent.

Highlighting an inconsistency in this past week's series was Bryce Molinaro. He’s been a productive player this season, with 11 home runs and 31 RBIs. However, he was hitless going into the final game of the series against Rutgers. But on Sunday, he had three hits, three runs and four RBIs, including a home run that was a part of a back-to-back-to-back triple home run set.

Clear consistencies

Someone who shined through the whole week was Jack Porter. He started his week with the only run scored against the Mountaineers; he also had more hits than the rest of his squad combined, with three.

This continued when Penn State returned to Happy Valley. He began the series with two runs and four RBIs on four hits. Then, in the second low-scoring matchup of the week, he was the only runner to score again. Back to the shootouts, he only had one hit, but managed to score three runs and have two RBIs. Porter is third on the team in RBIs and hits, as well as tied for second in runs; he continues to be a highlight for the blue and white.

Another consistency has been pitcher Dimond Loosli. He saw action in two games this week, earning a win in the series finale against Rutgers. He first pitched against West Virginia, throwing just one inning against West Virginia, allowing no hits with two strikeouts. Then in his win, he allowed no hits again, with one strikeout in the same amount of innings.

Penn State will have to learn to be consistent if it wants to play in the Big Ten Tournament.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Matt Campbell talks Blue-White Practice, injury updates
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
It’s a big week for Penn State, with the NFL draft kicking off on Thursday and Blue-White Practice on Saturday.

Amidst the chaos, Matt Campbell took to the podium in Lasch Building to talk about this weekend’s Blue-White Practice, the upcoming team schedule and injury recovery.

Here’s more of what Campbell had to say in his last weekly press conference of the spring.

Blue-White Practice

The team and fans alike will experience a new event at Beaver Stadium on Saturday, as Penn State hosts its spring game in the form of Blue-White Practice.

At Iowa State, Campbell hosted a scrimmage as the Cyclones’ spring game. However, Campbell explained this year’s event will run more like a typical Penn State practice during the first period.

“I kind of see the first 20-25 minutes like what a normal practice would look like,” Campbell said. “Team stretch and then obviously get ourselves a little bit of individual inside run, a little bit of 7-on-7.”

The rest of the event will operate as a typical spring game, with the Nittany Lions running offense and defense together with drives down the field.

“An opportunity to continue to work the football team and work on driving the football in different areas of the field,” Campbell said.

Summer plans

With spring ball ending with practice No. 15 on Saturday, Campbell and his staff have already made plans to keep the team progressing over the 12 weeks of practice this summer.

“The early part of next week is to individually, go through every player with everybody in our program, and get a synopsis of, ‘Where are these players right now? What’s their strengths, what’s their weaknesses?’” Campbell said.

Next week, Campbell hopes to lay out the entirety of summer training separately with each player, highlighting what each athlete needs to do over the next three months to succeed in the fall.

“If you can really evaluate your team now and have a chance to really go to work and put the right pieces together, I think it’ll give us the best opportunity moving forward,” Campbell said.

Injury status

Heading into spring ball, the Nittany Lions were without its two top quarterbacks and wide receivers, who were all recovering from injury.

As of Tuesday, quarterback Rocco Becht has continued to work 7-on-7 drills and backup Alex Manske has officially returned to campus after an unspecified procedure, according to Campbell.

“Obviously the biggest thing that’s been a positive is Rocco getting healthy,” Campbell said. “You’ll see him Saturday. I want him to throw the football in the stadium with our fans and everybody else.”

While Manske won’t play in the Blue-White Practice, Campbell is happy with his progress and expects him to return this summer.

“It’s huge, I think it’s a monumental win for us,” Campbell said. “He’s certainly trending towards where we would want him to be by the time we get to the summer so he can progress his way back.”

As receivers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen continue to work their way back from injury, they’ve had the opportunity to practice individual reps before making their way into playing with the whole team.

“Honestly, it’s been awesome for so many guys to get the foundation of what we’re doing on both sides of the ball,” Campbell said. “Our coaches have done a great job, and honestly, our kids have done a great job. We haven’t wasted a practice.”

Has also spent time at UIC and Xavier.Averaged 2.1PPG and 1.9RPG this season. Averaged 9.9PPG, 7.6RPG in… pic.twitter.com/JFg8tO7JGJ— Sam Kayser (@KayserHoops) April 21, 2026
